report_content:
Date of Birth: 1954

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
I21.4 Acute subendocardial myocardial infarction
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 162/98 mmHg; Pulse: 94/min; SpO2: 96%; Temp: 36.7°C; VAS: 7 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Chest pain and pressure
Anamnesis
History: Long-standing arterial hypertension, treated with amlodipine. Hypercholesterolemia, poorly controlled. Former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 2 years ago). No previous cardiac interventions or known coronary artery disease. Family history positive for early myocardial infarction (father at age 52).
Current Illness: Patient reports sudden onset of retrosternal pressure and "burning" sensation that began approximately 4 hours ago while gardening. The pain radiates to the left shoulder and lower jaw. Associated with mild nausea and diaphoresis. He initially took an antacid at home thinking it was indigestion, with no relief.
Functions: Stool and urination normal. No recent weight changes.
Habits: Does not consume alcohol.
Medications: Amlodipine 5 mg, Atorvastatin 20 mg.
Allergies: Denied.
Status
Of medium musculoskeletal build, slightly overweight. Conscious, communicative, oriented. Patient appears anxious and is mildly diaphoretic. Eupneic at rest. Skin and visible mucous membranes are of normal color.
Neck: No jugular venous distention, no carotid bruits.
Lungs: Auscultation reveals normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Regular rhythm, tones somewhat muffled, no murmurs. BP 158/95 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. Audible peristalsis.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. Pulses palpable and symmetric.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus rhythm, rate 92/min, intermediate electrical axis. Horizontal ST-segment depression of 1.5 mm in leads V4, V5, and V6. Flattened T-waves in I and aVL. No pathological Q-waves.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.92 [1e12]/L, HGB 148 g/L, Hct 0.442 L/L, MCV 89.8 fL, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, NEU% 74.2 %, CRP 5.2 mg/L.
Urea 6.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 82 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
High-sensitivity Troponin I (hs-TnI): 942.0 ng/L (Reference range: < 26.2 ng/L)
CK-MB: 42 U/L (Reference range: < 24 U/L)
Glucose: 7.2 mmol/L.
(Dr. Hrvoje Vidović, 18.07.2024 10:15)
Summary Image of Thoracic Organs (AP):
Lungs are clear of infiltrates or congestion. The hila are of normal size and configuration. The cardiothoracic ratio is at the upper limit of normal. Costophrenic angles are sharp.
(Dr. Marina Kos, 18.07.2024 10:45)
Therapy
0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (1 dose)
Diazepam 5 mg IM
Conclusion
Based on clinical presentation, ECG changes (ST depression in lateral leads), and significant elevation of cardiac enzymes (Troponin I 942 ng/L), the patient is presenting with an acute NSTEMI. He remains hemodynamically stable.
Admission to the Department of Internal Medicine, Cardiology Unit, for further monitoring and coronary angiography.

(Dr. Jakov Reljić, 18.07.2024 11:30)
gold_response:
<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Missing intervention
QUOTE: "Therapy: 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion, Pantoprazole 40 mg IV, Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (1 dose), Diazepam 5 mg IM"
PROBLEM: The patient has a confirmed NSTEMI (positive troponin and ST-segment depression) but was not administered antiplatelet therapy (Aspirin and a P2Y12 inhibitor) or anticoagulation (e.g., Heparin or Enoxaparin). These are the standard-of-care treatments required immediately to prevent thrombus propagation and further myocardial infarction.
ACTION: Immediately administer a loading dose of Aspirin (162–325 mg) and a P2Y12 inhibitor (e.g., Ticagrelor or Clopidogrel), and initiate parenteral anticoagulation.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Contraindicated route of administration. The use of an intramuscular (IM) injection (Diazepam) is discouraged in patients with acute myocardial infarction who require anticoagulation or potential thrombolysis due to the risk of hematoma formation.
TYPE: Missing risk stratification. There is no documented calculation of a validated risk score (e.g., GRACE or TIMI), which is essential for determining the timing of the invasive coronary angiography (urgent vs. early).
TYPE: Inadequate statin management. The patient is currently on a low-intensity statin (Atorvastatin 20 mg); standard NSTEMI protocol requires the initiation of high-intensity statin therapy (e.g., Atorvastatin 80 mg) regardless of prior use.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: Therapy: 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion, Pantoprazole 40 mg IV, Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (1 dose), Diazepam 5 mg IM
error_explanation: The patient has a confirmed Acute Subendocardial Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI) based on high-sensitivity Troponin I levels (942 ng/L) and ischemic ECG changes (ST-segment depressions). However, the physician failed to administer or order any antiplatelet therapy (such as Aspirin or a P2Y12 inhibitor) or anticoagulation (such as Heparin or Enoxaparin), which are the fundamental standard of care for acute coronary syndromes.
correct_action: The patient should have immediately received a loading dose of Aspirin (160-325 mg), a P2Y12 inhibitor (e.g., Ticagrelor 180 mg or Clopidogrel 300-600 mg), and systemic anticoagulation (e.g., Unfractionated Heparin or Enoxaparin) prior to or upon admission to cardiology.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1984

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
N20.1 Calculus in ureter
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 158/94 mmHg; Pulse: 102/min; SpO2: 99%; Temp: 36.8°C; VAS: 9 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
18.07.2024, 14:20: BP 155/92, Pulse 98, SpO2 99%, Resp 18, Temp 36.7, VAS 9, GCS 15
Reason for Arrival
Severe right-sided flank pain radiating to the groin.

Anamnesis
History: Previous episode of nephrolithiasis in 2019, passed spontaneously. No known chronic kidney disease. History of mild seasonal allergies.
Current Illness: Patient reports sudden onset of agonizing, colicky pain in the right flank starting approximately 3 hours ago. The pain radiates downward toward the right inguinal region and scrotum. Associated with two episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. Denies fever, chills, or dysuria. Reports urgency to urinate but only passing small amounts of clear urine.
Functions and Habits: Regular bowel movements. No smoking history. Occasional social alcohol consumption. Appetite currently decreased due to nausea.
Medications: None regularly.
Allergies: Penicillin (hives).

Status
General: Patient appears in significant distress, unable to find a comfortable position ("pacing" the exam room). Conscious, oriented x3. Skin is diaphoretic but warm and well-perfused. No peripheral edema.
Head/Neck: Normocephalic. Mucous membranes slightly dry. No jugular venous distension.
Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Tachycardic but regular rhythm. Clear S1, S2, no murmurs or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. No guarding or rebound tenderness. Deep palpation reveals mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant but no peritoneal signs. Audible peristalsis. Positive right-sided costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness (Goldflam sign). Left side non-tender.
Extremities: Symmetrical, full range of motion, palpable distal pulses.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus tachycardia, 104/min, normal axis, no ST-T wave abnormalities.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L (slightly elevated), RBC 4.8 [1e12]/L, HGB 145 g/L, Hct 0.42 L/L, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L.
Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, Urea 6.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 92 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, CRP 2.4 mg/L.
Urinalysis: Appearance: Yellow, slightly turbid. pH 6.0, Specific Gravity 1.025. Protein: Negative. Glucose: Negative. Nitrites: Negative. Leukocyte Esterase: Negative. Erythrocytes: 3+ (Microscopic hematuria). Microscopy: 20-30 RBC/hpf, 1-2 WBC/hpf, no bacteria.
(Dr. Elena Vaskova, Clinical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 15:15)

Imaging:
Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) - Urinary Tract:
Right Kidney: Mild hydronephrosis and hydroureter observed. A hyperechoic structure measuring approximately 4 mm is visualized in the distal third of the right ureter near the vesicoureteric junction (VUJ) with posterior acoustic shadowing. No free fluid in the Morison pouch.
Left Kidney: Normal size and echogenicity, no hydronephrosis.
Bladder: Partially filled, no masses.
(Performed by Dr. Markovic Jovan, 18.07.2024 15:30)

Therapy Administered in ED
- Saline 0.9% 500 mL IV infusion
- Ketorolac 30 mg IV (Toradol)
- Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
- Ondansetron 4 mg IV

Conclusion
Clinical presentation, microscopic hematuria, and ultrasound findings are consistent with acute right-sided renal colic due to a distal ureteral calculus (4 mm). No evidence of urinary tract infection (afebrile, negative nitrites/leukocytes) or renal impairment (normal creatinine).

The patient's pain significantly improved (VAS 2/10) following intravenous analgesia. Given the stone size (<5 mm) and lack of complications, the patient is suitable for outpatient management (Medical Expulsive Therapy).

Recommendations:
1. Tamsulosin 0.4 mg once daily for 10 days to facilitate stone passage.
2. Ibuprofen 400-600 mg as needed for pain (not to exceed 1800 mg/day).
3. Increase fluid intake to 2.5-3 liters per day.
4. Strain urine to catch the stone for analysis if possible.
5. Follow-up with a urologist in 7 days with a repeat KUB X-ray or ultrasound.
6. Return to the ED immediately if: fever develops, pain becomes uncontrollable with oral medication, or inability to urinate occurs.

Discharged in stable condition.

Patient 4
MBOO: 284719302

Date of Birth: 1951

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
A09.9 Gastroenteritis and colitis of unspecified origin
R11 Nausea and vomiting
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 112/68 mmHg; Pulse: 94/min; SpO2: 97%; Temp: 36.9°C; VAS: 3; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Date	BP /mmHg	Pulse /min	SpO2 %	Resp /min	Temp °C	Guk mmol/L	VAS	GCS
18.07.2024 09:15	112/68	94	97%	18	36.9	5.8	3	15

Reason for Arrival
Nausea, vomiting, and general weakness.

Anamnesis
History: Long-standing arterial hypertension, benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), and chronic low back pain.
Current Illness: The patient reports a two-day history of nausea and frequent vomiting (4-5 times a day). He describes a dull, generalized abdominal discomfort but denies sharp pain or cramps. No diarrhea reported; last bowel movement was yesterday, normal consistency. He feels "very weak" and "unsteady on his feet." No fever or respiratory symptoms.
Functions: Poor appetite over the last 48 hours, decreased urine output. 
Habits: Non-smoker, social alcohol consumption (denies recent intake).
Medications: Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg 1+0+0, Amlodipine 5 mg 0+0+1, Omnic Ocas 0.4 mg, Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for back pain.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Status
Patient is of medium build, conscious, communicative, oriented to person and place, though appears slightly lethargic. Dehydrated appearance: dry oral mucosa, reduced skin turgor. Afebrile, eupneic at rest.
Head: Normocephalic, pupils equal and reactive to light.
Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, thyroid not enlarged.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Rhythmic, tones somewhat distant, no murmurs. BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 90/min.
Abdomen: Slightly distended, soft, mild diffuse tenderness on deep palpation, no rebound or guarding. No palpable masses. Peristalsis present but sluggish.
Extremities: No edema, peripheral pulses palpable. Neurologically: no gross motor deficits, but patient notes feeling "lightheaded" upon sitting up.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus rhythm, frequency 92/min, intermediate electrical axis, no acute ST-T changes.
Lab Results:
WBC 9.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.60 [1e12]/L, HGB 148 g/L, Hct 0.442 L/L, MCV 91.0 fL, MCH 31.2 pg, Platelets 215 [1e9]/L, CRP 4.2 mg/L.
Glucose 5.9 mmol/L, Urea 9.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 115 µmol/L, Na 121 mmol/L, K 3.6 mmol/L, Cl 89 mmol/L.
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, Medical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 10:45)

Radiographic Imaging of the Abdomen (Supine):
No signs of pneumoperitoneum. No dilated loops of small or large bowel suggestive of ileus. Moderate amount of fecal material in the colon. 
Conclusion: Non-specific finding.
(Dr. Sc. Goran Šimić, MD, 18.07.2024 11:20)

Therapy administered in ED:
- 500 ml 0.9% NaCl IV
- Reglan (metoclopramide) 10 mg IV

Conclusion
(Dr. Ana Knežević, 18.07.2024 13:30)
The patient's clinical presentation is consistent with viral gastroenteritis and mild dehydration. After receiving intravenous rehydration and antiemetics, the patient reports feeling subjectively better and the nausea has subsided. Abdominal findings remain benign.
The patient is discharged home. 
Recommendations: 
- Small, frequent sips of rehydration salts (Rehidromix).
- Light diet (tea, rusks, boiled rice).
- Discontinue Ibuprofen temporarily.
- Follow-up with the primary care physician in 2 days for control of renal function and electrolytes.
- In case of worsening symptoms or inability to keep fluids down, return to the ED.
gold_response:
<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Missed diagnosis / Unsafe discharge
QUOTE: "Na 121 mmol/L... The patient is discharged home."
PROBLEM: The patient has severe hyponatremia (Sodium 121 mmol/L), which is a potentially life-threatening electrolyte derangement that can lead to seizures, coma, or permanent neurological damage if not managed in an inpatient setting. Discharging an elderly, symptomatic (lethargic, unsteady) patient with this level of hyponatremia is a critical safety failure.
ACTION: The patient must be admitted to the hospital for cautious, monitored sodium correction and further investigation of the hyponatremia.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Failure to discontinue Hydrochlorothiazide. This medication is a common cause of severe hyponatremia in the elderly and should have been permanently stopped, yet it was not mentioned in the discharge instructions.
TYPE: Inadequate fluid resuscitation. A single 500 ml bolus of 0.9% NaCl is insufficient for a patient showing clinical signs of dehydration (dry mucosa, reduced turgor) and laboratory evidence of pre-renal azotemia (Urea 9.4 mmol/L).
TYPE: Failure to address falls risk. The patient is 73 years old, "unsteady on his feet," and "lightheaded upon sitting up," yet was discharged home without a gait assessment or falls risk intervention.
TYPE: Misdiagnosis of viral gastroenteritis. While vomiting occurred, the severe hyponatremia and thiazide use provide a more compelling primary explanation for the patient's lethargy and weakness than simple viral illness.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: Na 121 mmol/L
error_explanation: The patient has a sodium level of 121 mmol/L, which indicates severe hyponatremia. This is a critical lab value that can lead to seizures, cerebral edema, or coma. Despite this result, the patient was discharged with a diagnosis of simple gastroenteritis, and the severe electrolyte imbalance was neither addressed in the treatment (beyond standard saline) nor cited as a reason for admission.
correct_action: A sodium level of 121 mmol/L requires immediate hospitalization for controlled correction and further diagnostic workup (e.g., assessing volume status, urine osmolarity, and sodium). Hydrochlorothiazide, a known cause of hyponatremia, should have been permanently discontinued rather than just suggesting a temporary stop of Ibuprofen.

Diagnoses

Triage Cat: 2; BP: 118/76 mmHg; Pulse: 114/min; SpO2: 91% (Room Air); Temp: 37.2°C; RR: 26/min; VAS: 6-Moderate/Severe pain; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Anamnesis
Current Illness: Patient presents with sudden onset of shortness of breath and sharp, right-sided pleuritic chest pain that began approximately three hours ago while resting. Reports a "heavy" feeling in the left leg for the past 3 days. Denies cough, hemoptysis, or syncope.
Past Illnesses: Total left hip arthroplasty 14 days ago. Essential hypertension.
Family History: Father had a myocardial infarction at age 62.
Functions: Normal urination, regular bowel movements.
Habits: Smoker (10 cigarettes/day for 20 years). Does not consume alcohol.
Therapy: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg 1x1 (discontinued 3 days ago per patient's own decision).
Allergies: Denies.
Status
Musculoskeletal build: Average, alert, communicative, oriented in time and space, tachypneic, mildly distressed, afebrile. Skin and visible mucous membranes are pale, no cyanosis.
Lungs: Auscultation reveals clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. SpO2 91% on room air, improves to 97% on 2L O2 via nasal cannula.
Heart: Tachycardic rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. S1Q3T3 pattern not clearly visible. BP: 118/76 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly, audible peristalsis.
Limbs: Left lower leg is visibly edematous compared to the right (circumference +3.5 cm at the calf). Tenderness on deep palpation of the left calf. Homans' sign positive on the left. Peripheral pulses palpable.
EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 115/min, normal axis, inverted T waves in V1-V3.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.80 [1e12]/L, HGB 145 g/L, Hct 0.420 L/L, MCV 87.5 fL, Trc 210 [1e9]/L, D-dimer 3450 ng/mL (Ref: <500), Troponin T (hs-TnT) 48 ng/L (Ref: <14), NT-proBNP 950 pg/mL (Ref: <300), CRP 12.4 mg/L, Glucose 6.2 mmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 82 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, AST 32 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, LDH 280 U/L.
(LUKIC ANA mag.med.biochem., 22.08.2024 14:15)

Arterial Blood Gas (on 2L O2):
pH 7.46, pCO2 4.2 kPa, pO2 10.8 kPa, HCO3 22.4 mmol/L, BE -1.2.

Radiographic Imaging:
Chest X-Ray (AP): No infiltrates, no pleural effusion. Heart shadow within normal limits. Normal pulmonary vascularity.

CT Angiography of the Pulmonary Arteries (CTPA):
Filling defects observed in the right main pulmonary artery and the segmental branches of the right lower lobe, consistent with acute pulmonary embolism. No evidence of right ventricular enlargement (RV/LV ratio < 1.0).

Color Doppler Ultrasound of Lower Extremity Veins:
Non-compressible segment of the left popliteal vein and posterior tibial veins with absence of flow, consistent with acute deep vein thrombosis (DVT).
Dr. M. Horvat

Conclusion:
(Dr. MARIĆ IVAN, MD, 22.08.2024 16:30)
Patient presented with classic symptoms of pulmonary embolism (Wells Score 9.0: HR > 100, recent surgery, clinical signs of DVT, and PE being the most likely diagnosis). Diagnosis confirmed via CTPA and elevated D-dimer. Elevated Troponin T and NT-proBNP suggest mild myocardial strain, though the patient remains hemodynamically stable (PESI Class III - moderate risk).

Therapy initiated in ED:
- Oxygen 2L/min via nasal cannula
- Enoxaparin 80 mg (1 mg/kg) SC stat
- Normal Saline 500 ml IV bolus

Plan:
Admission to the Department of Pulmonology for further monitoring and initiation of oral anticoagulant therapy (Apixaban). Monitor for signs of hemodynamic instability.
Follow-up with vascular surgery for post-thrombotic syndrome prevention.

report_content:
Date of birth: 1961

Triage category: 3
Diagnoses
R42 Dizziness and giddiness
Triage Data
Triage category: 3; BP: 138/85 mmHg; Pulse: 62/min; SpO2: 99%; Temp: 36.5°C; VAS: 2 – Mild pain; GCS: 15
Vital signs
Date	BP /mmHg	Pulse /min	SpO2 %	Resp /min	Temp °C	Trect °C	Guk mmol/L	VAS	GCS	AVPU
18.07.2024 09:15	138/85	62	99%	14				2	15	A	
Reason for visit
Sudden onset of dizziness and generalized weakness.
History
CURRENT ISSUES: The patient reports the sudden onset of dizziness starting this morning upon waking. Describes a sensation of "spinning" that lasts for several seconds when turning his head to the right. Also reports feeling "heavy" in his legs and general malaise. Denies chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. No history of recent falls or head trauma. No tinnitus or hearing loss.
Medical History: Arterial hypertension for 10 years, Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH). 
Bowel movements and urination are normal.
Non-smoker, denies alcohol use.
Medications: Lisinopril 20 mg 1x1, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg 1x1.
Drug allergies: Penicillin (skin rash).
Status
Medium musculoskeletal build (92 kg), conscious, oriented x3. 
Mucous membranes moist, skin normally colored.
Neck: No carotid bruits, thyroid not enlarged.
Heart: Rhythmic heart action, clear tones, no murmurs. HR 62/min.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezes.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.
Neurological: No cranial nerve deficits. Sensation intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Finger-to-nose test normal. Romberg test slightly unstable. No nystagmus at rest.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
12-lead ECG: Sinus rhythm, rate 60/min, normal axis, no ST-T segment changes. PR interval 160ms.
Metabolites and Substrates:
Blood glucose 6.1 mmol/L; Urea 9.2 mmol/L; Creatinine 138 µmol/L; eGFR 51.4 mL/min/1.73m²; Total bilirubin 14 µmol/L
Electrolytes:
Na 140 mmol/L; **K 6.4 mmol/L**; Cl 102 mmol/L; Ca 2.25 mmol/L
Complete Blood Count:
WBC 8.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.51 [1e12]/L, HGB 142 g/L, Hct 0.421 L/L, Platelets 215 [1e9]/L
Proteins:
CRP 2.1 mg/L

Radiographic Imaging:
CT Head (Non-contrast):
Findings: No signs of acute intracranial hemorrhage, territorial infarction, or expansive process. Ventricular system is of normal size and shape. No midline shift.
(Dr. M. Horvat, 18.07.2024, 11:45)

Conclusion
The clinical presentation and diagnostic workup (negative CT head, normal neurological exam) are suggestive of peripheral vestibular dysfunction, likely Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV). The patient reports feeling slightly better after a period of observation. No acute cerebrovascular event was identified. The patient is stable for discharge. 
Recommendation: Follow up with a primary care physician for further vestibular rehabilitation or ENT consultation. 
Therapy: Betahistine 24 mg twice daily.
(Dr. I. Perić, MD, 18.07.2024, 12:30)
gold_response:
<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Missing intervention
QUOTE: "K 6.4 mmol/L; ... The patient is stable for discharge."
PROBLEM: The patient was discharged with severe hyperkalemia (Potassium 6.4 mmol/L), which is a life-threatening electrolyte abnormality that requires immediate treatment to prevent cardiac arrest. The patient’s symptoms of "generalized weakness" and "heavy legs" are clinical manifestations of hyperkalemia, which were incorrectly attributed solely to BPPV.
ACTION: The patient must be immediately recalled to the Emergency Department for cardiac monitoring and urgent potassium-lowering therapy (e.g., calcium gluconate, insulin/dextrose, and potassium binders).
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Missed medication side effect: The patient is taking Lisinopril (an ACE inhibitor) while having impaired renal function (eGFR 51.4), which is the likely cause of the severe hyperkalemia.
TYPE: Incomplete physical examination: A Dix-Hallpike maneuver was not documented to confirm the diagnosis of BPPV, despite "spinning" being a primary complaint.
TYPE: Failure to address abnormal renal labs: The elevated Creatinine (138 µmol/L) and Urea (9.2 mmol/L) were not addressed in the conclusion or discharge plan.
TYPE: Inappropriate medication continuation: Lisinopril should have been suspended or discontinued in the presence of hyperkalemia and renal impairment.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: K 6.4 mmol/L
error_explanation: The patient has a potassium level of 6.4 mmol/L, which indicates severe hyperkalemia. This is a critical laboratory value that can lead to life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias or cardiac arrest. The error is compounded by the fact that the patient is taking Lisinopril (an ACE inhibitor), which is known to cause potassium retention, and has a reduced eGFR (51.4), indicating impaired renal clearance. Despite this critical finding, the discharge conclusion only addresses the vertigo symptoms and fails to mention, treat, or follow up on the hyperkalemia.
correct_action: The physician should have recognized the critical hyperkalemia, ordered an immediate repeat lab to confirm (to rule out hemolysis), performed a repeat ECG to look for hyperkalemic changes (peaked T-waves), and initiated treatment (e.g., calcium gluconate for membrane stabilization, insulin/glucose or beta-agonists to shift potassium intracellularly, and potentially sodium polystyrene sulfonate or loop diuretics). The patient should have been admitted for cardiac monitoring rather than discharged.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1945

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses

N18.4 Chronic kidney disease, stage 4

Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 108/62 mmHg; Pulse: 74/min; SpO2: 95%; Temp: 36.6°C; VAS: 6 - Moderate pain; GCS: 15;

Vital Signs
Stable at time of examination. Orthostatic vitals: BP 105/60 (sitting), 92/54 (standing - patient reported dizziness).

Reason for Arrival
Fall following a dizzy spell.

Anamnesis
History: Long-standing arterial hypertension, Chronic Kidney Disease (Stage 4, baseline Creatinine ~190-210 µmol/L), and Heart Failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF, last Echo EF 50%). History of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH).
Current Illness: Patient reports that this morning, after standing up quickly from the breakfast table, he felt a "sudden darkness" and lightheadedness. He fell to the floor, landing on his right side. Denies loss of consciousness, tongue biting, or urinary incontinence. He currently complains of significant pain in the right hip and lower back area. No chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath prior to the event.
Functions: Appetite slightly decreased lately. Stool regular. Urination frequent at night (nocturia 3x).
Habits: Former smoker (quit 10 years ago). Occasional glass of wine.
Medications: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Furosemide 40 mg 1x1, Bisoprolol 2.5 mg 1x1, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg 1x1, Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (skin rash).

Status
Elderly male, appears stated age, conscious, communicative, oriented x3. Eupneic at rest.
Head/Neck: No signs of trauma to the skull. Carotids symmetrical without bruits. Jugular veins not distended.
Lungs: Basal crackles bilaterally, otherwise clear breath sounds.
Heart: Distant heart sounds, regular rhythm, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses. Bowel sounds present.
Extremities: Right hip is tender to palpation over the greater trochanter. No visible shortening or external rotation of the limb. Range of motion limited by pain. Mild 1+ pitting edema of the lower legs. Peripheral pulses palpable.
Neurological: No focal neurological deficits. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Romberg test not performed due to hip pain.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
EKG: Sinus rhythm, 72/min, left axis deviation, no acute ST-T wave changes.
X-ray Right Hip/Pelvis: No evidence of acute fracture or dislocation. Severe osteoarthritic changes noted in both hip joints.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 6.8 [1e9]/L, RBC 3.42 [1e12]/L, HGB 108 g/L, Hct 0.321 L/L, MCV 93.8 fL, MCH 31.5 pg, MCHC 336 g/L, Platelets 184 [1e9]/L.
Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, Urea 16.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 212 µmol/L, eGFR 26 mL/min/1.73m², Na 136 mmol/L, K 4.9 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L, CRP 4.2 mg/L, Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL (negative), NT-proBNP 1250 pg/mL.

Conclusion
The syncopal episode is most likely orthostatic in nature, possibly exacerbated by current diuretic therapy and underlying autonomic dysfunction. Cardiac origin (arrhythmia) is less likely given the EKG and lack of prodromal palpitations, but cannot be entirely ruled out. Right hip pain is secondary to contusion/soft tissue injury; no fracture was visualized on X-ray.
The patient is hemodynamically stable. Adjusted medication regimen recommended.

Recommendations:
1. Increase oral fluid intake and rise slowly from a supine/sitting position.
2. Reduce Furosemide to 20 mg daily; follow up with primary care physician for blood pressure and renal function monitoring in 3 days.
3. For management of acute hip pain, take Ibuprofen 400 mg every 8 hours for the next 5 days.
4. Follow up with a cardiologist for an elective Holter EKG.
5. If new fainting spells, chest pain, or inability to bear weight occurs, return to the ED immediately.

Report signed by: Dr. Julian Vance, MD
Date: 22.10.2024
gold_response:
<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Contraindicated medication
QUOTE: "For management of acute hip pain, take Ibuprofen 400 mg every 8 hours for the next 5 days."
PROBLEM: The patient has Stage 4 Chronic Kidney Disease (eGFR 26 mL/min). NSAIDs like Ibuprofen are contraindicated in advanced CKD as they can cause acute-on-chronic kidney injury and fluid retention, potentially exacerbating his heart failure.
ACTION: Discontinue Ibuprofen and substitute with Acetaminophen (Paracetamol) for pain management.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Medication Interaction ("Triple Whammy"). The combination of an ACE inhibitor (Ramipril), a diuretic (Furosemide), and an NSAID (Ibuprofen) significantly increases the risk of acute renal failure.
TYPE: Failure to address Tamsulosin. Tamsulosin is an alpha-blocker that frequently causes orthostatic hypotension in elderly patients; its contribution to the syncopal event was not addressed in the medication adjustments.
TYPE: Risk of Heart Failure Exacerbation. NSAIDs promote sodium and water retention, which is dangerous for a patient with documented diastolic heart failure and basal crackles.
TYPE: Incomplete Syncope Workup. While orthostasis is likely, the patient has Stage 4 CKD and HFpEF; a basic screening for electrolyte imbalances beyond the provided labs (e.g., Magnesium/Calcium) or a more formal orthostatic challenge (3-minute stand) should be ensured before discharge.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: For management of acute hip pain, take Ibuprofen 400 mg every 8 hours for the next 5 days.
error_explanation: The patient has Stage 4 Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) with an eGFR of 26 mL/min/1.73m² and is also taking an ACE inhibitor (Ramipril) and a diuretic (Furosemide). NSAIDs like Ibuprofen are strictly contraindicated in advanced CKD as they can cause further decline in renal function, promote fluid retention (exacerbating his heart failure), and increase the risk of acute-on-chronic kidney injury through the inhibition of prostaglandins that maintain renal perfusion.
correct_action: Avoid NSAIDs. Use Acetaminophen (Paracetamol) as the first-line analgesic, or consider topical NSAIDs or low-dose opioids if pain is severe and not controlled by Acetaminophen, while monitoring renal function closely.

Palpitations and mild shortness of breath.
Anamnesis
History: Long-standing essential hypertension treated for 15 years. No history of coronary artery disease, diabetes, or prior stroke. This is the first episode of palpitations.
Current Illness: Patient reports sudden onset of "racing heart" and "fluttering" in the chest starting approximately 4 hours ago while resting. Accompanied by mild shortness of breath upon walking. Denies chest pain, syncope, or focal neurological deficits.
Functions and Habits: Normal bowel movements and urination. Non-smoker, does not consume alcohol. Appetite is normal.
Medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Amlodipine 5 mg daily.
Allergies: Penicillin (skin rash).
Status
Patient is an elderly female of medium build, fully conscious, communicative, and oriented to person, place, and time. Eupneic at rest, afebrile, non-icteric, skin and mucous membranes are well-perfused.
Chest: Symmetrical respiratory movements.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Irregular rhythm, tachycardic, heart sounds are distinct, no murmurs or gallops. Peripheral pulses are irregular and palpable.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses or organomegaly. Peristalsis is present.
Extremities: No pretibial edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 112/min. Normal electrical axis. No ST-segment elevation or depression. No pathological Q waves.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 6.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.25 [1e12]/L, HGB 132 g/L, Hct 0.395 L/L, MCV 93.0 fL, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, NEU 62%, LYM 28%.
Sodium 139 mmol/L, Potassium 4.1 mmol/L, Chloride 102 mmol/L, Urea 6.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 82 µmol/L, eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m², Glucose 5.4 mmol/L.
Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL (Negative).
TSH 1.45 mIU/L (Normal).
(Dr. Elena Vukas, Clinical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 14:15)
Conclusion
The patient presents with a first-time episode of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Symptoms were managed in the ED with a single dose of Metoprolol 25 mg orally, resulting in a reduction of heart rate to 82/min (still irregular).
Regarding stroke prophylaxis, the CHA2DS2-VASc score was calculated as 1 (1 point for Hypertension). Based on this score, the risk of thromboembolism is low, and oral anticoagulation is not indicated at this time.
The patient is discharged with a prescription for Aspirin 100 mg daily and Bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily.
Urgent follow-up with a cardiologist is recommended within 7 days for consideration of cardioversion or further rhythm management.
Report to the emergency department immediately if experiencing chest pain, severe shortness of breath, or signs of stroke (facial drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty).

gold_response:

FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Wrong risk calculation
QUOTE: "CHA2DS2-VASc score was calculated as 1 (1 point for Hypertension). Based on this score, the risk of thromboembolism is low, and oral anticoagulation is not indicated at this time."
PROBLEM: The CHA2DS2-VASc score was incorrectly calculated. The patient is 76 years old (2 points), female (1 point), and has hypertension (1 point), resulting in a total score of 4, which indicates a high risk for thromboembolism.
ACTION: The patient must be started on oral anticoagulation (e.g., a DOAC like Apixaban or Rivaroxaban) immediately, as the threshold for treatment is a score of ≥2 in females.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Inappropriate medication (Aspirin for AF). Aspirin is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation as it is significantly less effective than anticoagulants and still carries a bleeding risk.
TYPE: Missed age-related risk. The clinician failed to account for the patient's age (76), which is a major risk factor (2 points) in the CHA2DS2-VASc scoring system.
TYPE: Omission of sex-related risk. The "Female" category in the CHA2DS2-VASc score was ignored, which adds 1 point to the total risk profile.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: the CHA2DS2-VASc score was calculated as 1 (1 point for Hypertension). Based on this score, the risk of thromboembolism is low, and oral anticoagulation is not indicated at this time. The patient is discharged with a prescription for Aspirin 100 mg daily
error_explanation: The physician miscalculated the CHA2DS2-VASc score. The patient is a 76-year-old female (Age >= 75 = 2 points; Female sex = 1 point) with Hypertension (1 point). The correct CHA2DS2-VASc score is 4, not 1. A score of 4 carries a high risk of stroke and necessitates oral anticoagulation (like Apixaban or Warfarin). Aspirin is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation.
correct_action: The CHA2DS2-VASc score should be correctly calculated as 4. The patient should be started on an oral anticoagulant (DOAC or Vitamin K antagonist) unless there are absolute contraindications, and Aspirin should not be used as a substitute for stroke prophylaxis in this context.

gold_response:
<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Missed diagnosis
QUOTE: "Laboratory findings show normal inflammatory markers and stable renal function. Based on the resolution of symptoms and the normal EKG findings, there is no indication for emergency hospitalization at this time."
PROBLEM: The physician failed to recognize a critically elevated high-sensitivity Troponin T (hs-TNT) of 142.8 ng/L, which is more than 10 times the typical upper limit of normal (~14 ng/L). This indicates acute myocardial injury and, in the context of the patient's symptoms, is diagnostic of a Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI).
ACTION: The patient must be immediately recalled, admitted to a monitored bed, started on ACS (Acute Coronary Syndrome) protocols (e.g., anticoagulation, antiplatelets), and evaluated by cardiology for urgent coronary angiography.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Failure to perform serial cardiac biomarkers. Even if the physician misinterpreted the first troponin, standard of care for chest-related symptoms (including shortness of breath/palpitations) requires a second troponin 1-3 hours later to assess delta changes.
TYPE: Inadequate risk stratification. The patient’s age (68) and history of hypertension/hyperlipidemia, combined with the elevated troponin, would result in a high HEART score, making discharge contraindicated.
TYPE: Failure to address the etiology of the "sudden onset" palpitations. While the EKG showed sinus rhythm at discharge, the significantly elevated troponin suggests the "fluttering" may have been a sustained tachyarrhythmia (like Atrial Fibrillation with RVR) or a primary ischemic event.
TYPE: Misleading clinical conclusion. The summary states laboratory findings are unremarkable except for inflammatory markers and renal function, which is a "negative mention" error that overlooks the most critical abnormal lab value (troponin) in the record.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: hs-TNT 142.8 ng/L
error_explanation: The high-sensitivity Troponin T (hs-TNT) is critically elevated at 142.8 ng/L (typical upper limit of normal is <14 ng/L). This finding is highly suggestive of acute myocardial injury or NSTEMI, even in the absence of EKG changes. The physician's conclusion completely ignores this result, stating that laboratory findings show no indication for hospitalization and discharging the patient.
correct_action: The patient should have been admitted for serial troponin monitoring, started on ACS (Acute Coronary Syndrome) protocols (anticoagulation, antiplatelets), and consulted with cardiology for potential urgent coronary angiography.

gold_response:
<thinking>
The patient is a 76-year-old male with multiple comorbidities, including chronic atrial fibrillation managed with Warfarin (Martefarin). He is diagnosed with a urinary tract infection (UTI) and prescribed Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim).

The most critical safety concern is the major drug-drug interaction between **Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) and Warfarin**. 
1.  **Mechanism:** Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim inhibits the CYP2C9 enzyme, which is responsible for metabolizing the more potent S-enantiomer of warfarin. It also displaces warfarin from protein-binding sites.
2.  **Clinical Impact:** This interaction almost universally leads to a significant elevation in the International Normalized Ratio (INR), often within 2–5 days of starting the antibiotic. This puts the patient at high risk for life-threatening hemorrhage (e.g., intracranial or gastrointestinal bleeding).
3.  **Management Error:** The physician explicitly instructed the patient to "Continue all home medications, including Warfarin 5 mg, at the current dose" without scheduling an early INR re-check (usually recommended within 48–72 hours) or preemptively reducing the warfarin dose.

Secondary concerns include:
*   **Male UTI Classification:** UTIs in males are considered "complicated" by definition, especially in the context of BPH. A 5-day course of Bactrim is generally considered insufficient; 7–14 days is the standard recommendation to prevent recurrence or progression to prostatitis.
*   **Hyperkalemia Risk:** The combination of Trimethoprim (which acts like a potassium-sparing diuretic) and Lisinopril (an ACE inhibitor) increases the risk of hyperkalemia, particularly in an elderly patient with a borderline eGFR (64 mL/min).
*   **Penicillin Allergy:** While the patient has a penicillin allergy (rash), Bactrim is a sulfonamide, so there is no direct cross-reactivity, but the choice of antibiotic remains problematic due to the warfarin interaction.
</thinking>

TYPE: Drug interaction
QUOTE: "Continue Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) 800/160 mg twice daily for a total of 5 days. Continue all home medications, including Warfarin 5 mg, at the current dose."
PROBLEM: Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim is a potent inhibitor of warfarin metabolism; co-administration without a dose reduction or immediate INR monitoring significantly increases the risk of supratherapeutic INR and life-threatening bleeding.
ACTION: Select an alternative antibiotic with less interaction potential (e.g., a 3rd generation Cephalosporin, given the penicillin allergy is only a rash) or mandate an INR check within 48-72 hours and consider a preemptive 25-50% reduction in the warfarin dose.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Suboptimal treatment duration; 5 days of antibiotics is typically insufficient for a male UTI (complicated UTI), where 7-14 days is standard.
TYPE: Potential hyperkalemia; the combination of Trimethoprim and Lisinopril in an elderly patient increases the risk of elevated potassium levels.
TYPE: Missing monitoring instructions; the discharge plan fails to specify an urgent INR re-test despite the high-risk drug combination.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: Continue Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) 800/160 mg twice daily for a total of 5 days. Continue all home medications, including Warfarin 5 mg, at the current dose.
error_explanation: Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9, the primary enzyme responsible for metabolizing the more active S-enantiomer of Warfarin. Co-administration typically results in a significant and rapid increase in the INR, substantially increasing the risk of life-threatening hemorrhage.
correct_action: The clinician should have either selected a different antibiotic (e.g., Nitrofurantoin or a Cephalosporin, given the penicillin allergy) or, if Bactrim was absolutely necessary, proactively reduced the Warfarin dose by 25-50% and scheduled a repeat INR check within 48 to 72 hours.

FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Missing intervention
QUOTE: "ECG: Sinus bradycardia, rate 52/min. Notable for... tall, symmetrically peaked T waves... Laboratory Results: K 6.9 mmol/L... Therapy: 0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV infusion over 2 hours"
PROBLEM: The patient has life-threatening hyperkalemia (6.9 mmol/L) with associated ECG changes (peaked T waves and bradycardia), but the physician failed to order emergent membrane stabilization or potassium-shifting agents. This presentation carries a high risk of imminent cardiac arrest.
ACTION: Immediately administer IV Calcium Gluconate (or Calcium Chloride) for myocardial membrane stabilization, followed by IV Insulin with Dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Missing diagnostic test (VBG/Lactate). Given the patient's Stage 3 AKI and continued Metformin use, there is a high risk of Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA), which requires urgent evaluation via venous blood gas and lactate levels.
TYPE: Inappropriate disposition. A patient with severe hyperkalemia and ECG changes requires continuous cardiac monitoring and immediate stabilization in a high-dependency or resuscitation area, rather than standard admission to a nephrology ward.
TYPE: Inadequate fluid resuscitation strategy. While 0.9% NaCl was started, the use of large volumes of normal saline in severe hyperkalemia can worsen metabolic acidosis and potentially exacerbate hyperkalemia; a balanced crystalloid may be more appropriate.
TYPE: Failure to address bradycardia. The bradycardia is a sign of potassium toxicity; while fluid is necessary for the AKI, the heart rate should be monitored closely as a marker of worsening electrolyte-induced conduction delay.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: Therapy: 0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV infusion over 2 hours, Pantoprazole 40 mg IV, Discontinuation of Ramipril, Hydrochlorothiazide, and Metformin
error_explanation: The patient has severe, life-threatening hyperkalemia (K+ 6.9 mmol/L) with corresponding ECG changes (peaked T waves). The clinician failed to order emergent membrane stabilization (e.g., Calcium Gluconate) and intracellular potassium shifting agents (e.g., Insulin with Dextrose, Beta-agonists, or Sodium Bicarbonate).
correct_action: The physician should have immediately administered 10-20 mL of 10% Calcium Gluconate IV to stabilize the myocardium, followed by 10 units of regular insulin with 50mL of 50% Dextrose to shift potassium into the cells, and potentially a loop diuretic or potassium binder.

TYPE: Inadequate follow-up timeframe. For a patient with an eGFR of 14 and recent severe hyperkalemia, a 3-5 day follow-up is dangerously delayed; if not admitted, they would require re-evaluation within 24 hours.

She reports a productive cough with yellowish sputum but denies high fever. She has a known history of COPD (Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease Stage II) and chronic heart failure (NYHA Class II). 
She notes that her usual rescue inhaler (Salbutamol) has provided minimal relief over the last 12 hours. She feels dizzy when standing up but denies syncope, chest pain, or focal neurological deficits.
Bowel movements are regular; urination is normal.
Smoker (retired, 40 pack-years), denies alcohol.

The patient is in atrial fibrillation with RVR, likely triggered by an exacerbation of COPD and mild heart failure decompensation. In the emergency department, she received oxygen (2L via nasal cannula), a nebulized bronchodilator (Ipratropium/Salbutamol), and 40 mg of intravenous Furosemide.

gold_response:
<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Drug interaction
QUOTE: "Amiodarone 200 mg 3x1 for 7 days... Continue home medications: Lanoxin (Digoxin) 0.25 mg 1x1."
PROBLEM: Amiodarone is a potent inhibitor of P-glycoprotein and significantly increases serum digoxin levels (often by 70-100%). In a 72-year-old patient with impaired renal function (eGFR 46.2), this combination without a dose reduction carries a very high risk of life-threatening digoxin toxicity.
ACTION: The digoxin dose should be reduced by 50% or discontinued when initiating amiodarone, with close monitoring of serum digoxin levels and electrolytes.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Missing intervention: Failure to initiate or address anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation despite a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4 (Female, Age 65-74, CHF, HTN), indicating a high risk of thromboembolism.
TYPE: Missing intervention: Failure to prescribe antibiotics for a COPD exacerbation characterized by purulent (yellowish) sputum and elevated CRP (28.5 mg/L), which meets GOLD criteria for antibiotic therapy.
TYPE: Contraindicated medication: The use of Amiodarone as a first-line rate control agent in a stable patient is generally reserved for those who cannot tolerate or have contraindications to beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers.
TYPE: Monitoring: Discharging an elderly patient with orthostatic symptoms ("dizzy when standing") immediately after administering IV diuretics and starting a high-dose amiodarone loading regimen increases the risk of falls and electrolyte-induced arrhythmias.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: Amiodarone 200 mg 3x1 for 7 days, then 1x1 daily. Continue home medications: Lanoxin (Digoxin) 0.25 mg 1x1
error_explanation: The report prescribes a new regimen of Amiodarone while instructing the patient to continue her current dose of Digoxin (0.25 mg). Amiodarone is a potent inhibitor of P-glycoprotein, which significantly reduces the renal and non-renal clearance of digoxin. This interaction typically increases serum digoxin concentrations by 50% to 100%, leading to a high risk of digoxin toxicity (arrhythmias, bradycardia, nausea, and visual disturbances), especially in an elderly patient with reduced eGFR (46.2 mL/min).
correct_action: When initiating Amiodarone in a patient already taking Digoxin, the Digoxin dose should be reduced by 30% to 50% empirically, and serum digoxin levels should be closely monitored over the following weeks.

gold_response:
<thinking>
1.  **Analyze the Patient Profile:** 69-year-old female with a history of Chronic Atrial Fibrillation (on Warfarin), Hypertension (on Ramipril), and Type 2 Diabetes. She has a Penicillin allergy (rash).
2.  **Identify the Acute Presentation:** Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) symptoms (dysuria, frequency, suprapubic pain) and palpitations.
3.  **Review Clinical Data:**
    *   Vitals: Stable, mild tachycardia (92-95 bpm), borderline temperature (37.9°C).
    *   Labs: Elevated WBC (12.4) and CRP (45), urinalysis positive for nitrites/leukocytes.
    *   Coagulation: INR is currently therapeutic at 2.3.
    *   ECG: Confirms AF with stable rate.
4.  **Evaluate Treatment and Decision-Making:**
    *   The physician diagnosed a UTI and prescribed **Bactrim (Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim)**.
    *   The physician instructed the patient to **continue Warfarin 5mg daily** at the current dose.
5.  **Identify Critical Safety Error:**
    *   **Drug-Drug Interaction (DDI):** Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9, the enzyme responsible for metabolizing the more active S-isomer of Warfarin. It also displaces Warfarin from protein binding sites and can alter gut flora that produce Vitamin K.
    *   **Clinical Consequence:** This interaction almost universally causes a significant, rapid increase in INR, often leading to supratherapeutic levels (>5.0 or even >10.0) and a high risk of life-threatening bleeding (e.g., intracranial hemorrhage or gastrointestinal bleed).
    *   **Management Failure:** The record explicitly tells the patient to continue her current Warfarin dose and follow up in 7 days. This is an unsafe interval for a patient starting Bactrim.
6.  **Identify Secondary Concerns:**
    *   **NSAID Use:** The patient uses Ibuprofen occasionally. NSAIDs significantly increase the risk of GI bleeding when combined with Warfarin and increase the risk of acute kidney injury (AKI) when combined with ACE inhibitors (Ramipril). The discharge instructions do not address the need to avoid NSAIDs during this acute illness.
    *   **Diagnosis Inconsistency:** The history states "Chronic AF," but the diagnosis listed is "Paroxysmal AF." While not immediately harmful, this reflects poor diagnostic precision.
</thinking>

ACTION: The antibiotic should be changed to a safer alternative (e.g., Nitrofurantoin or Fosfomycin) or, if Bactrim is mandatory, the Warfarin dose must be empirically reduced by 25-50% with an INR re-check scheduled within 48-72 hours.

error_explanation: The physician prescribed Bactrim (Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim) to a patient taking Warfarin without adjusting the Warfarin dose or scheduling an immediate INR follow-up. Bactrim is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9, the primary enzyme responsible for metabolizing the more active S-isomer of Warfarin. This interaction significantly increases the anticoagulant effect of Warfarin, often causing a rapid and dangerous rise in INR, which puts the patient at high risk for major or life-threatening hemorrhage.

Medical History:
Patient has a history of essential hypertension for 10 years, managed with ACE inhibitors. Diagnosed with hypercholesterolemia in 2021. History of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). No previous surgical history. 
Current Illness:
The patient presents to the emergency department complaining of sudden onset epigastric "burning" and pressure that began approximately 4 hours ago while gardening. The pain radiates slightly to the lower sternum and left shoulder. He reports feeling nauseated and has had one episode of non-bilious emesis. He took an over-the-counter antacid with minimal relief. He denies fever, cough, or shortness of breath. He notes that the pain feels "different" than his usual reflux.
Function:
    • Appetite: Normal until today
    • Weight Loss: None
    • Stool: Normal, last this morning
    • Urination: Normal
Habits:
    • Smoker: Yes, 1 pack per day for 30 years
    • Alcohol: Occasional social drinking
Therapy:
    • Lisinopril 10 mg 1x1
    • Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1
    • Pantoprazole 20 mg as needed
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status:
    • Musculoskeletal build: Ectomorphic, appears uncomfortable and slightly diaphoretic. Conscious, cooperative, oriented x3. Skin is cool to the touch. No peripheral lymphadenopathy. Neck veins not distended.
    • Chest: Symmetrical, normal respiratory excursions.
    • Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.
    • Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, S1/S2 heard clearly, no murmurs or gallops.
    • Abdomen: Soft, mild tenderness on deep palpation in the epigastric region, no guarding or rebound tenderness. No organomegaly. Audible peristalsis.
    • Limbs: No edema, peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetrical.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:
    • EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 106/min. Horizontal ST-segment depression of 1mm in leads V4, V5, and V6. No Q waves.
    • Blood tests: WBC 9.4 x10^9/L, RBC 4.88 x10^12/L, HGB 142 g/L, HCT 0.421 L/L, MCV 86.3 fL, Platelets 210 x10^9/L, NEU% 68.2%.
    • Chemistry: Glucose 7.2 mmol/L, Urea 5.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 82 mL/min/1.73m², AST 42 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, GGT 45 U/L, LDH 290 U/L, CRP 3.2 mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L.
    • Cardiac Markers: hs-TNT (High-sensitivity Troponin T) 58.4 ng/L (Reference range <14 ng/L).
    • Abdominal Ultrasound: Gallbladder wall of normal thickness, no stones. Liver and spleen within normal limits. Minimal gas interference in the epigastrium.
Therapy:
    • Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
    • Reglan 10 mg IV
    • Saline 0.9% 500 ml IV infusion
Conclusion:
Patient presented with epigastric distress and nausea. After administration of IV proton pump inhibitor and antiemetic therapy, the patient reports significant improvement in the "burning" sensation (VAS reduced to 2/10). The mild EKG changes are likely chronic or related to tachycardia and the patient's known hypertension. Elevated troponin is noted but may be related to early hypertensive demand. Given the symptomatic relief with GI-directed therapy, the clinical picture is most consistent with acute gastritis/GERD exacerbation. 
Plan: Discharge home. Continue Pantoprazole 40 mg 1x1 for 14 days. Follow up with primary care physician for outpatient stress test and gastroenterology consult if symptoms persist. Return to ED if pain becomes crushing or if severe shortness of breath occurs.

gold_response:
<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Missed diagnosis
QUOTE: "The mild EKG changes are likely chronic... Elevated troponin is noted but may be related to early hypertensive demand. Given the symptomatic relief with GI-directed therapy, the clinical picture is most consistent with acute gastritis/GERD exacerbation. Plan: Discharge home."
PROBLEM: The patient meets the clinical, electrocardiographic, and biochemical criteria for a Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI). Discharging a patient with a positive high-sensitivity Troponin T (4x the upper limit of normal), ST-segment depression in precordial leads, and exertional pain radiating to the shoulder is a life-threatening error.
ACTION: Rescind discharge, initiate Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) protocol (aspirin, anticoagulation), and admit to a monitored bed for cardiology consultation and urgent coronary angiography.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Failure to administer standard of care ACS medications (Aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors, or Heparin) despite an elevated troponin.
TYPE: Misinterpretation of "symptomatic relief" from GI medications as a rule-out for cardiac ischemia (the "GI cocktail" fallacy).
TYPE: Failure to perform serial troponin testing to evaluate the delta (change) in hs-TNT levels.
TYPE: Dismissal of ischemic EKG changes (ST depression) as "chronic" without comparison to a prior EKG in a symptomatic patient.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: Conclusion: ...the clinical picture is most consistent with acute gastritis/GERD exacerbation. Plan: Discharge home.
error_explanation: The physician overlooked a clear diagnosis of Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI). The patient presents with classic 'anginal equivalent' symptoms (epigastric pain radiating to the shoulder, nausea, diaphoresis) and has significant risk factors (smoking, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia). Critically, the hs-TNT is 58.4 ng/L (over 4x the upper limit of normal) and the EKG shows ST-segment depression in the precordial leads. Attributing these findings to gastritis because the patient felt better after IV fluids and a PPI is a common and dangerous cognitive bias (premature closure).
correct_action: The patient should have been admitted to the Cardiac Care Unit (CCU) or a monitored telemetry bed for an ACS (Acute Coronary Syndrome) protocol, started on antiplatelet therapy (Aspirin/Ticagrelor), and scheduled for urgent coronary angiography.

gold_response:
<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Missed diagnosis
QUOTE: "The chest pain is likely musculoskeletal in origin, possibly exacerbated by the patient's change in gait and posture following recent knee surgery. The tachycardia and mild hypoxia are attributed to pain and anxiety."
PROBLEM: The physician failed to diagnose a highly probable Pulmonary Embolism (PE). The patient presents with a classic triad: recent orthopedic surgery (14 days ago), pleuritic chest pain with hypoxia (SpO2 93%), and a positive D-dimer (1.85 mg/L), yet was discharged with "anxiety" and "myalgia" as explanations for life-threatening physiological markers.
ACTION: The patient must be stabilized, started on empiric anticoagulation (unless contraindicated), and undergo an urgent CT Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA).
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Misinterpretation of hypoxia. Attributing a SpO2 of 93% to "anxiety" is clinically unsound; anxiety and hyperventilation typically cause normal or elevated oxygen saturation, whereas hypoxia suggests a V/Q mismatch such as PE.
TYPE: Failure to investigate DVT. The physical exam noted a 2 cm difference in calf circumference in a post-operative patient, which is a significant clinical marker for Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) that should have prompted a lower extremity venous duplex ultrasound.
TYPE: Incorrect use of Wells' Criteria/PERC. The patient has a Wells' Score of at least 3.0 (tachycardia + recent surgery), making them "moderate risk," and they fail the PERC rule; in both algorithms, a positive D-dimer necessitates definitive imaging (CTPA or V/Q scan).
TYPE: Dangerous discharge. Discharging a tachycardic, hypoxic patient with an unexplained elevated D-dimer and recent surgical history carries a high risk of sudden death from a saddle or massive pulmonary embolism.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: Conclusion: The chest pain is likely musculoskeletal in origin... The tachycardia and mild hypoxia are attributed to pain and anxiety.
error_explanation: The physician failed to investigate Pulmonary Embolism (PE) in a high-risk patient. The patient has a high Wells Score (recent surgery/immobilization, tachycardia, and clinical suspicion of DVT/calf swelling) and has clear clinical markers (hypoxia with SpO2 93%, pleuritic chest pain, and a significantly elevated D-dimer of 1.85 mg/L). Relying on a normal Chest X-ray to rule out PE is a classic error, as X-rays are often normal in PE cases ('Westermark sign' or 'Hampton's hump' are rare).
correct_action: The physician should have performed a CT Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA) or a V/Q scan to rule out pulmonary embolism before discharge, especially given the post-operative status and elevated D-dimer.

12.10.2024 09:15	102/64	108	95%	20	38.9	6.4	3	14	V
Reason for visit
Fever, urinary frequency, and confusion.
History
CURRENT ISSUES: The patient is a 75-year-old female brought to the Emergency Department by her daughter due to a three-day history of painful urination and increased frequency. Since yesterday evening, the patient has become increasingly somnolent and "confused," according to the daughter. She has had a poor oral intake of fluids over the last 48 hours and has not voided since this morning. 
Comorbidities: Arterial hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (well-controlled), and Osteoarthritis.
Previous Lab Results (August 2024): Serum Creatinine was 82 µmol/L.
Medications: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Metformin 500 mg 2x1, Meloxicam 15 mg as needed.
Drug allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status
Aged female, appearing acutely ill, somnolent but arousable to verbal stimuli. 
Mucous membranes: Dry, tongue coated. Skin turgor decreased.
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. BP 105/68 mmHg, HR 104/min.
Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Abdomen: Soft, significant tenderness on suprapubic palpation. No guarding or rebound. Bilateral renal angles non-tender.
Limbs: No edema, peripheral pulses weak but palpable.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
12-lead ECG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 106/min, no ST-T segment changes.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 18.4 x10^9/L (High), RBC 4.10 x10^12/L, HGB 128 g/L, Platelets 210 x10^9/L.
Metabolites and Substrates:
Blood glucose 7.2 mmol/L; Urea 18.4 mmol/L (High); Creatinine 218 µmol/L (High - Baseline 82); eGFR 20.4 mL/min/1.73m² (Low).
Electrolytes:
Na 132 mmol/L (Low); K 5.1 mmol/L; Cl 98 mmol/L.
Inflammatory markers:
CRP 164 mg/L (High).
Urinalysis:
Cloudy, Protein 1+, Nitrites positive, Leukocyte esterase 3+, Bacteria many, WBC >50/hpf.

Ultrasound of the Kidneys and Bladder:
Kidneys are of normal size and cortical thickness. No signs of hydronephrosis or urolithiasis. Bladder is nearly empty.
(Dr. I. Horvat, MD, 12.10.2024, 11:45)
Conclusion
The patient presents with a documented urinary tract infection and systemic inflammatory response (fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis). Laboratory findings are significant for Acute Kidney Injury (AKI Stage 2), likely pre-renal due to dehydration and sepsis. During the stay in the ED, the patient received 500ml of Normal Saline. The patient remains somewhat lethargic but is able to swallow. Given the lack of obstructive uropathy on ultrasound, the patient is stable for outpatient management. It is recommended to start oral Cefuroxime 500mg twice daily for 10 days. Increase oral fluid intake. Discontinue Ramipril and Meloxicam until renal function recovers. Follow-up with a primary care physician in 48 hours for repeat creatinine.

Dr. J. Marković

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1965

Diagnoses
E87.5 Hyperkalemia
N18.4 Chronic kidney disease, stage 4
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension

Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; BP: 128/74 mmHg; Pulse: 48/min; SpO2: 97%; Temp: 36.6°C; VAS: 2-Mild pain; GCS: 15

Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Generalized muscle weakness and palpitations.

Anamnesis
Current Illness: A 59-year-old patient with a known history of stage 4 chronic kidney disease presents with a two-day history of progressive generalized weakness, particularly in the lower extremities, and a sensation of "skipped heartbeats." The patient missed his last scheduled outpatient nephrology appointment. He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or syncope. He reports decreased urine output over the last 24 hours. No fever or cough.
Past Illnesses: Chronic Kidney Disease (Stage 4, non-dialysis dependent currently), Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Arterial Hypertension, Gout.
Family History: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 62.
Functions: Reduced appetite, nausea without vomiting. Obstipation for 3 days.
Habits: Ex-smoker (quit 10 years ago). Does not consume alcohol.
Therapy: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Furosemide 40 mg 1x1, Amlodipine 10 mg 1x1, Allopurinol 100 mg 1x1, Insulin glargine 18 units at bedtime.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Status
The patient is conscious, communicative, and oriented. Appears slightly fatigued.
Skin: Pale, dry, no signs of dehydration or edema.
Head/Neck: Oropharynx clear, neck veins not distended. No carotid bruits.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezes.
Heart: Bradycardic, regular rhythm, tones slightly muffled. No murmurs. BP 128/74 mmHg. Pulse 48/min.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses. Bowel sounds diminished.
Extremities: Symmetrical, no pretibial edema. Strength 4/5 in lower extremities bilaterally, 5/5 in upper extremities. Sensation intact.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
EKG: Sinus bradycardia, rate 48/min. Notable for tall, peaked T-waves in leads V2-V6 and a slightly widened QRS complex (118 ms). No ST-segment elevation.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 8.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 3.42 [1e12]/L, HGB 104 g/L, Hct 0.312 L/L, Platelets 190 [1e9]/L.
Glucose 7.2 mmol/L.
Urea 24.5 mmol/L, Creatinine 485 µmol/L (Baseline 310 µmol/L), eGFR 12 mL/min/1.73m².
Sodium 134 mmol/L, **Potassium 7.1 mmol/L**, Chloride 102 mmol/L, Calcium 2.10 mmol/L.
CRP 8.4 mg/L.
Troponin T (hs-TnT): 18 ng/L (within normal limits for CKD).

Conclusion:
(Dr. M. Horvat, MD, 22.10.2024 14:20)
The patient presents with symptoms of weakness and bradycardia associated with acute-on-chronic renal failure and significant hyperkalemia. EKG shows typical changes of electrolyte imbalance (peaked T-waves). 
The patient was observed in the emergency department for 3 hours. During observation, the patient remained hemodynamically stable. 

The patient is discharged home. He is advised to strictly adhere to a low-potassium diet and to discontinue his Ramipril immediately. He must contact his nephrologist tomorrow morning to arrange for urgent outpatient dialysis or a medication review. 
Prescribed Polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) 15g to be taken orally tonight.
In case of chest pain or fainting, return to the ED immediately.

gold_response:

FOUND: Yes
TYPE: Inappropriate disposition and missing emergency intervention
QUOTE: "The patient is discharged home. He is advised to strictly adhere to a low-potassium diet... Prescribed Polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) 15g to be taken orally tonight."
PROBLEM: The patient has severe, life-threatening hyperkalemia (7.1 mmol/L) with associated EKG changes (peaked T-waves, widened QRS) and symptomatic bradycardia, which constitutes a medical emergency. Discharging this patient without immediate stabilization and inpatient monitoring carries a high risk of cardiac arrest or sudden death.
ACTION: The patient must be immediately readmitted/retained for emergent stabilization (calcium gluconate, insulin/dextrose) and urgent hemodialysis.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Failure to administer cardiac membrane stabilization (e.g., Calcium Gluconate) despite EKG changes (widened QRS and peaked T-waves).
TYPE: Failure to administer intracellular potassium-shifting therapies (e.g., Insulin with Glucose, Albuterol) to acutely lower serum potassium.
TYPE: Contraindicated use of Kayexalate in a patient with suspected bowel obstruction or significant constipation ("Obstipation for 3 days"), which increases the risk of intestinal necrosis.
TYPE: Failure to recognize that Kayexalate has a slow onset of action (hours to days) and is inappropriate as a monotherapy for acute, severe hyperkalemia.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: The patient is discharged home. He is advised to strictly adhere to a low-potassium diet and to discontinue his Ramipril immediately. He must contact his nephrologist tomorrow morning to arrange for urgent outpatient dialysis or a medication review.
error_explanation: The patient has severe, life-threatening hyperkalemia (Potassium 7.1 mmol/L) with associated EKG changes (peaked T-waves, bradycardia, widened QRS). This is a medical emergency. The physician failed to administer immediate stabilizing interventions such as intravenous calcium gluconate to stabilize the myocardium, and shifting agents like insulin/dextrose or nebulized albuterol. Furthermore, discharging a patient with a potassium level of 7.1 and EKG changes is a critical failure in disposition; the patient requires immediate hospitalization and potentially emergent hemodialysis.
correct_action: The patient should have been stabilized with IV calcium gluconate, IV insulin with glucose, and potentially sodium bicarbonate or albuterol. He should have been admitted to a monitored bed (ICU or Step-down) and an urgent nephrology consultation should have been obtained for emergent hemodialysis.

PROBLEM: The patient has severe hyperkalemia (6.4 mmol/L) with associated EKG changes (bradycardia, PR prolongation, and peaked T-waves), which is a life-threatening medical emergency. Discharging the patient without stabilization or treatment carries a high risk of sudden cardiac arrest.
ACTION: Immediate recall of the patient for emergency stabilization (IV calcium, insulin/glucose) and inpatient cardiac monitoring.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Contraindicated discharge medications. The physician stopped Hydrochlorothiazide (a potassium-wasting diuretic) while continuing Lisinopril and Spironolactone, both of which will further increase the patient's dangerously high potassium levels.
TYPE: Misattribution of syncope. The syncopal event was likely a result of hyperkalemia-induced bradyarrhythmia rather than vasovagal or orthostatic causes, given the pulse of 52-54 and EKG changes.
TYPE: Failure to recognize CKD progression. The patient’s eGFR has dropped to 31 mL/min (Stage 3b), making the combination of Lisinopril and Spironolactone highly risky; these should have been held or discontinued immediately.
TYPE: Inappropriate triage/disposition. A patient with symptomatic bradycardia and a potassium level of 6.4 mmol/L requires a higher level of acuity (Triage Category 1 or 2) and mandatory admission for nephrology consultation.
</other_errors>

error_location: K 6.4 mmol/L
error_explanation: The patient has a potassium level of 6.4 mmol/L, which constitutes severe hyperkalemia. This is a life-threatening condition that can lead to fatal cardiac arrhythmias, especially given the patient's existing bradycardia and EKG changes (prominent T-waves, borderline PR prolongation). The patient is also on Lisinopril and Spironolactone, both of which raise potassium, and has impaired renal function (eGFR 31). Discharging the patient without immediate treatment is a critical error.
correct_action: The patient should have been stabilized immediately with intravenous calcium (to protect the heart), followed by therapies to shift potassium into cells (insulin with glucose, nebulized albuterol) and measures to remove potassium from the body. Antihypertensive medications like Lisinopril and Spironolactone should have been held, and the patient should have been admitted for continuous cardiac monitoring until the potassium level was safely corrected.

TYPE: Misinterpretation of "GI cocktail" response: Improvement of epigastric pain after PPI/antacids does not rule out myocardial infarction, as cardiac pain can occasionally diminish coincidentally or via placebo effect.
TYPE: Failure to perform serial troponin testing: Even if the initial troponin were borderline, a second test should have been performed to assess for a rising or falling pattern (delta).
TYPE: Failure to address ECG abnormalities: The "non-specific" ST-segment depressions in V5-V6 are clinically significant when paired with an elevated troponin and epigastric pain in a 65-year-old male.
TYPE: Inappropriate discharge of an unstable patient: Discharging a patient with biochemical evidence of myocardial injury (positive troponin) and ongoing risk factors is a critical safety failure that carries a high risk of mortality.

Epigastric pain is a common 'atypical' presentation of myocardial ischemia, especially in patients with cardiovascular risk factors like hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Date of Birth: 1974

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
K81.0 Acute cholecystitis
K80.0 Calculus of gallbladder with acute cholecystitis
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 148/92 mmHg; Pulse: 98/min; SpO2: 98%; Temp: 37.9°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Severe pain under the right rib cage, nausea, and vomiting.
Anamnesis
History: Patient has a history of dyslipidemia and obesity (BMI 32). No previous abdominal surgeries. Known to have "sludge" in the gallbladder found during a routine check-up 2 years ago.
Current Illness: The patient reports sudden onset of sharp, cramping pain in the right upper quadrant (RUQ) that began approximately 6 hours ago after a heavy dinner. The pain radiates to the right scapula. She has vomited twice since the onset. She reports feeling "feverish" but did not take her temperature at home. Last bowel movement was yesterday, normal consistency. No urinary complaints.
Functions: Appetite currently absent due to nausea. Normally regular stools. No weight loss.
Habits: Non-smoker. Social alcohol consumption (occasional glass of wine).
Medications: Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1, Ibuprofen as needed for headaches.
Allergies: Penicillin (reported skin rash).
Status
Patient is of robust musculoskeletal build, obese, conscious, communicative, and oriented. Appears in acute distress due to pain. Slightly febrile to the touch, eupneic at rest, non-icteric. Skin and visible mucous membranes are well-perfused. Tongue is slightly dry and coated.
Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. BP 145/88 mmHg. Pulse 102/min.
Abdomen: Above the level of the chest (obese), soft, but with significant tenderness and guarding in the right upper quadrant. Positive Murphy's sign. No rebound tenderness in other quadrants. No palpable masses. Audible peristalsis, slightly diminished. Bilateral lumbar percussion painless.
Extremities: Symmetrical, no edema, peripheral pulses present and equal.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 104/min, normal axis, no acute ST-T wave changes.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 14.8 [1e9]/L, Neutrophils 82%, RBC 4.92 [1e12]/L, HGB 142 g/L, Hct 0.42 L/L, Platelets 280 [1e9]/L.
CRP 52.4 mg/L.
Bilirubin total 22 µmol/L (slightly elevated), Bilirubin direct 8 µmol/L, AST 38 U/L, ALT 42 U/L, GGT 65 U/L, ALP 115 U/L, Amylase (serum) 68 U/L, Lipase 40 U/L.
Urea 5.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 82 µmol/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Glucose 6.2 mmol/L.
Abdominal Ultrasound:
The gallbladder is distended with a thickened wall (5.5 mm) and shows a "double contour" sign. Multiple gallstones are visualized, the largest being 14 mm, impacted in the gallbladder neck. Positive sonographic Murphy's sign. Pericholecystic fluid is present. The common bile duct (CBD) is not dilated (diameter 4.5 mm). Intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. Liver, spleen, and pancreas appear normal for the patient's habitus.
Consultation:
Surgical consult (Dr. Ivan Horvat): Clinical and ultrasound findings are consistent with acute calculous cholecystitis. Indication for surgical admission and laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
Therapy administered in ED:
- 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion
- Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
- Metoclopramide 10 mg IV
- Ketorolac 30 mg IV
- Ceftriaxone 2g IV (after confirming allergy profile; patient's Penicillin allergy noted, but cross-reactivity risk assessed and Ceftriaxone administered under monitoring as per local protocol for non-anaphylactic reactions, or alternatively Ciprofloxacin 400mg IV + Metronidazole 500mg IV if caution preferred. Decision made for Ciprofloxacin/Metronidazole due to rash history).
Conclusion
The patient presents with clinical, laboratory, and sonographic signs of acute calculous cholecystitis. There are no signs of common bile duct obstruction (normal CBD, near-normal LFTs) or pancreatitis (normal lipase). The patient was stabilized in the ED with IV fluids, analgesics, and antiemetics. Antibiotic therapy was initiated.
The patient is being admitted to the Department of General Surgery for further treatment and planned laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
NPO (nothing by mouth) status maintained.

Dr. Ana Kovač, MD

She has a history of Grade I obesity and essential hypertension, for which she takes perindopril. She denies any previous abdominal surgeries. No history of diabetes or chronic kidney disease.

Current Illness:
The patient reports the sudden onset of sharp, constant pain in the right upper quadrant (RUQ) of the abdomen approximately 6 hours ago, shortly after eating a dinner containing fried food. The pain radiates to the right scapula. She has vomited twice since the pain began. She reports feeling "feverish" and chilled at home. She denies any changes in stool color or dark urine. No chest pain or shortness of breath.

Function:

    • Chest: Symmetrical, normal respiratory excursion.
    • Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.
    • Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. BP 145/88 mmHg, pulse 102/min.
    • Abdomen: Slightly distended, soft. Marked tenderness to palpation in the right upper quadrant. Positive Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on deep palpation of the RUQ). Mild voluntary guarding present; no rebound tenderness. Bowel sounds are diminished but present. No palpable masses or organomegaly.

        AST 32 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, GGT 52 U/L, Alkaline Phosphatase 115 U/L (normal range), Total Bilirubin 16 µmol/L (normal), Amylase 45 U/L.
        Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
    • Abdominal Ultrasound:

Multiple mobile hyperechoic structures with posterior acoustic shadowing are seen within the lumen, the largest measuring 18 mm. Pericholecystic fluid is present. Sonographic Murphy's sign is positive. The common bile duct (CBD) measures 4 mm in diameter, with no evidence of choledocholithiasis. Liver, spleen, and pancreas appear morphologically normal within the limits of the examination.

Conclusion:
The patient's clinical presentation (RUQ pain, positive Murphy's sign, fever), laboratory findings (leukocytosis, elevated CRP), and ultrasound results (gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, stones) are consistent with acute calculous cholecystitis. Acute pancreatitis and common bile duct obstruction have been ruled out by normal amylase, bilirubin, and CBD diameter.

A surgical consultation was obtained (Dr. M. Horvat). The patient is planned for admission to the Department of Surgery for further management and laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The patient is to remain NPO (nothing by mouth).

________________________________________
