o	3 mm calculus in the distal right ureter near the ureterovesical junction.  
o	Mild right hydroureter and mild hydronephrosis.  
o	No perinephric stranding suggestive of infection.  
o	No other acute abdominal pathology identified.  
(Reviewed with radiology; timestamp 06.02.2026, 03:55)  
________________________________________  
Therapy (ED Course)  
•	IV access, monitoring.  
•	Analgesia: Ketorolac 30 mg IV once.  
•	Antiemetic: Ondansetron 4 mg IV once.  
•	Hydration: 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV.  
•	After imaging confirmation and symptom improvement:  
o	Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO given in ED (medical expulsive therapy).  

Response: Pain decreased from VAS 9/10 to 3/10; nausea resolved. Hemodynamically stable throughout observation. No fever. Able to tolerate oral fluids prior to discharge.  
________________________________________  
Conclusion / Disposition  
Assessment:  
•	Clinical presentation, urinalysis (hematuria without signs of infection), and CT confirm uncomplicated right distal ureteral stone (3 mm) with mild hydronephrosis.  
•	No evidence of obstructive pyelonephritis (afebrile, normal CRP, no pyuria, nitrite negative, no systemic toxicity). Renal function preserved.

Discharge Plan and Recommendations:  
•	Outpatient management appropriate due to symptom control, stable vitals, preserved renal function, and no infection.  
•	Medications:  
o	Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain (take with food; avoid other NSAIDs concurrently).  
o	Paracetamol 1,000 mg PO every 8 hours as needed (max 3,000 mg/day if self-medicating).  
o	Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO once daily for up to 14 days (stop earlier if stone passes and symptoms resolve; caution for dizziness/orthostasis).  
o	Ondansetron 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea.  
•	Advise oral hydration (drink to maintain light-yellow urine; avoid forced excessive fluids if nauseated).  
•	Strain urine to capture stone for analysis.  
•	Urology follow-up within 7–10 days (earlier if persistent pain) and repeat imaging per urology to confirm passage if not observed.  

Return to ED / call EMS immediately for:  
•	Fever/chills, worsening flank pain not controlled with medication, persistent vomiting/inability to keep fluids down, decreased urination, fainting, or any new concerning symptoms.

Patient received verbal and written instructions and demonstrated understanding.

| 06.02.2026 18:31 | 138/84 | 92 | 99 | 16 | 36.7 | 5 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 20:20 | 128/78 | 84 | 99 | 16 | 36.6 | 2 | 15 |

---

## Reason for Visit
**Cut to right hand with persistent bleeding and pain**

---

## History
### Current illness
Patient presents after cutting the **right palm** while washing dishes; reports a glass broke and he “caught it” reflexively. Injury occurred ~45 minutes prior to arrival. Initial bleeding was brisk; controlled at home with direct pressure but resumes when pressure released.

Denies syncope, chest pain, dyspnea, or other injuries. Denies numbness but notes pain with finger flexion.

**Tetanus history:** *“Last tetanus shot was about 12 years ago; not sure.”*

### Past medical history
- No chronic diseases reported.
- No known bleeding disorders.

### Medications
- None.

### Allergies
- Denies drug allergies.

### Habits / social
- Occasional alcohol on weekends; non-smoker.
- Works as a waiter.

---

## Physical Examination
**General:** Awake, oriented, mildly anxious due to bleeding; hemodynamically stable; afebrile.  
**Skin:** Well perfused.  
**Right hand (dominant):**  
- **3.2 cm linear laceration** on the **palmar aspect**, ulnar side, just proximal to MCP region.  
- Oozing bleeding; no pulsatile bleeding.  
- Wound edges clean but reported glass exposure.  
- **Neurovascular:** Capillary refill <2 s in all digits; radial/ulnar pulses palpable. Sensation to light touch intact in all digits.  
- **Motor:** Full active ROM of fingers; pain on flexion of 4th/5th digit but strength preserved.  
- No gross tendon exposure seen.  
**Other systems:** No acute findings.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
### Imaging
**X-ray right hand (PA/oblique/lateral):**
- No fracture.
- No radiopaque foreign body detected.
- Joint alignment preserved.

### Laboratory results (ED panel)
**Hematology – CBC:**
- WBC **8.9 ×10⁹/L**
- RBC **4.78 ×10¹²/L**
- Hemoglobin **146 g/L**
- Hematocrit **0.432 L/L**
- MCV **90.4 fL**
- Platelets **214 ×10⁹/L**

**Differential:**
- Neutrophils **64%** (5.7 ×10⁹/L)
- Lymphocytes **26%** (2.3 ×10⁹/L)
- Monocytes **7%** (0.6 ×10⁹/L)
- Eosinophils **2%** (0.2 ×10⁹/L)
- Basophils **1%** (0.1 ×10⁹/L)

**Biochemistry / inflammation:**
- Glucose **5.4 mmol/L**
- Urea **5.2 mmol/L**
- Creatinine **79 µmol/L** (eGFR **>90 mL/min/1.73 m²**)
- CRP **2.1 mg/L**
- Na **140 mmol/L**, K **4.1 mmol/L**, Cl **104 mmol/L**
- Ca **2.33 mmol/L**

### Consultations
- No specialist consultation requested (hand function intact; no fracture/foreign body on X-ray).

---

## Therapy / Procedures in ED
- Wound cleaned with **copious irrigation (0.9% NaCl)**; antisepsis performed.
- **Local anesthesia:** Lidocaine 1% (without adrenaline), total **6 mL** infiltrated.
- **Exploration:** No visible glass fragments; no tendon exposure.
- **Closure:** **5 simple interrupted sutures** (nylon 4-0).
- **Dressing:** Sterile non-adherent dressing + light compressive wrap.
- **Analgesia:** Ibuprofen **400 mg PO** given in ED.
- **Antibiotic (prophylaxis due to glass-contaminated palmar wound):** Amoxicillin/clavulanate **875/125 mg PO**, first dose given; prescription for **1 tab q12h x 5 days**.

---

## Discharge Condition
Improved; pain controlled; no active bleeding through dressing. Neurovascular status intact before and after suturing.

---

## Recommendations / Instructions
- Keep dressing clean/dry for 24 hours; thereafter daily wound care with clean dressing.
- Elevate hand and limit heavy use for 48–72 hours.
- Return immediately for: increasing pain, redness, swelling, pus, fever, numbness/tingling, decreased finger movement, or recurrent bleeding.
- **Suture removal in 10–12 days** (family doctor / outpatient clinic).
- Continue prescribed antibiotic course.
- OTC analgesia as needed (ibuprofen/paracetamol within recommended dosing).

---

## Conclusion
Right palmar laceration without fracture or radiopaque foreign body. Wound irrigated and sutured in ED; patient discharged in good general condition with oral antibiotics and wound care instructions.

- Missing intervention: No immobilization described—consider protective splinting (e.g., intrinsic-plus or per local hand protocol) and activity restriction to reduce risk of tendon disruption or wound dehiscence, especially if deeper injury is not definitively excluded.  
- Missed diagnosis: Retained glass foreign body not completely ruled out—X‑ray can miss small fragments; consider bedside ultrasound if suspicion persists, and ensure explicit follow-up/return precautions for persistent focal pain, swelling, or drainage.
</other_errors>

error_explanation: A contaminated laceration (glass injury) in a patient whose last tetanus vaccination was >10 years ago requires tetanus prophylaxis per standard wound management. ________________________________________

<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes  
TYPE: Wrong risk calculation  
QUOTE: "Patient discharged home with oral antibiotics (amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125 mg 2x1 for 7 days) and instructed to continue inhalers, hydrate, and return if worse."  
PROBLEM: This patient meets high-risk criteria for severe community-acquired pneumonia/sepsis and is not safe for discharge: persistent hypotension (~96/54 after fluids), lactate 3.1 mmol/L with mottling/decreased urine, and ongoing acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (SpO₂ 93% on O₂; PaO₂ 62 mmHg on 3 L/min) with marked work of breathing. CURB-65 is at least 3 (Age ≥65, Urea 11.4, Diastolic BP ≤60), which generally warrants inpatient management (often HDU/ICU-level monitoring depending on response).  
ACTION: Admit urgently for monitored care (consider HDU/ICU), continue IV antibiotics, complete sepsis management (repeat lactate, adequate fluids, vasopressors if needed), and provide escalation-ready respiratory support.  
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
Missing intervention: Incomplete sepsis resuscitation—only 1 L fluids despite ongoing hypotension and lactate elevation; no documented repeat lactate, urine output monitoring, or vasopressor plan if MAP remains low after adequate fluid challenge.  
Missing intervention: Inadequate outpatient antibiotic plan if discharge were considered—amoxicillin/clavulanate alone does not continue atypical coverage; the single azithromycin dose given in ED is insufficient for CAP with comorbidities (needs a full macrolide/doxy course or alternative regimen).  
Contraindicated medication: Advising continuation of home meds without adjustment—metformin should typically be held in acute sepsis/hypoperfusion or rising lactate/AKI risk to reduce lactic acidosis risk; ACE inhibitor (ramipril) may also need temporary hold in hypotension/possible AKI.  
Missing intervention: Respiratory management escalation not addressed—persistent tachypnea/fatigue and PaO₂ 62 on supplemental O₂ should prompt consideration of HFNC/NIV readiness, closer monitoring, and treatment of possible concurrent COPD exacerbation (e.g., systemic steroids if clinically indicated) rather than discharge.  
</other_errors>

error_location: “Patient discharged home with oral antibiotics (amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125 mg 2x1 for 7 days) and instructed to continue inhalers, hydrate, and return if worse.”
error_explanation: The patient meets high-risk criteria requiring admission: hypoxemic respiratory failure (SpO2 89% on room air; PaO2 62 mmHg on supplemental O2), persistent tachypnea, hypotension (~96/54) after fluids, elevated lactate (3.1 mmol/L) and markedly elevated inflammatory markers consistent with sepsis/poor perfusion. Discharging a patient who still requires supplemental oxygen and has unstable vitals risks rapid deterioration, respiratory failure, and septic shock.
correct_action: Admit to hospital (at least monitored ward; consider step-down/ICU depending on response), continue oxygen with titration and repeat ABG/pulse oximetry, administer scheduled IV antibiotics per CAP/sepsis protocol, repeat lactate and vitals, evaluate for sepsis source control and complications (parapneumonic effusion), and document formal internal medicine/pulmonology assessment.

report_content:
**Emergency Department Report**

**Patient**: Male, 68 years  
**Date of Birth**: 1957-11-18  
**Date/Time of Arrival**: 2026-02-06 14:12  
**Mode of Arrival**: Ambulance (home pickup)

---

## Triage

**Triage Category**: 2 (very urgent)  
**Chief Complaint**: Fever, productive cough, shortness of breath, near-syncope.

**Triage Data (14:15)**  
- BP: 88/56 mmHg  
- Pulse: 118 /min  
- SpO₂: 89% (room air)  
- Respiratory Rate: 32 /min  
- Temperature: 39.1 °C  
- GCS: 15  
- VAS: 3/10 (pleuritic right-sided chest pain when coughing)

### Vital Signs (serial)
| Date/Time | BP (mmHg) | Pulse (/min) | SpO₂ (%) | RR (/min) | Temp (°C) | GCS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 06.02.2026 14:15 | 88/56 | 118 | 89 (RA) | 32 | 39.1 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 14:35 | 94/60 | 112 | 94 (O₂ 3 L NC) | 28 | 38.7 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 15:25 | 102/66 | 104 | 95 (O₂ 2 L NC) | 24 | 38.2 | 15 |

---

## Reason for Admission
Progressive dyspnea and fever for 2 days with productive cough; today near-syncope on standing.

---

## Anamnesis

**Current Illness**:  
Two days of fever, chills, malaise, increasing cough with yellow sputum. Increasing shortness of breath on minimal exertion. Today felt dizzy and almost fainted when walking to the bathroom. Reports pleuritic pain in right lower chest with coughing. No hemoptysis. No calf swelling/pain. No recent surgery or prolonged travel.

**Past Medical History**:  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (on oral therapy)  
- Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), former smoker  
- Arterial hypertension  
- Chronic kidney disease stage 2 (baseline creatinine ~110 µmol/L per patient)

**Epidemiological History**: Household contacts with “flu-like illness” in past week.

**Habits**: Former smoker (35 pack-years, quit 5 years ago). Rare alcohol.

**Home Medications**:  
- Metformin 1000 mg 1-0-1  
- Empagliflozin 10 mg 1-0-0  
- Ramipril 5 mg 1-0-0  
- Tiotropium inhaler 1 puff daily  
- Salbutamol inhaler PRN

**Allergies**: No known drug allergies.

---

## Physical Examination (14:25)

**General**: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic, tachypneic, speaking in short sentences. Oriented x3.  
**Skin/Mucosa**: Warm, dry mucosa. Capillary refill ~3 seconds.  
**Neck**: No meningism.  
**Lungs**: Increased work of breathing. Decreased breath sounds and coarse crackles over right lower lung field; scattered wheeze bilaterally.  
**Heart**: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs.  
**Abdomen**: Soft, non-tender, no guarding.  
**Extremities**: No unilateral leg swelling, no calf tenderness. Peripheral pulses palpable.  
**Neuro**: No focal deficits.

---

## Diagnostics

### ECG (14:28)
Sinus tachycardia ~120/min. No ST elevation. No acute ischemic changes.

### Arterial Blood Gas (on room air, 14:32)
- pH 7.46  
- pCO₂ 4.2 kPa  
- pO₂ 7.3 kPa  
- SaO₂ 90%  
- HCO₃⁻ 22 mmol/L  
- Lactate 3.1 mmol/L

### Laboratory Tests (14:40)
**CBC**
- WBC 18.6 ×10⁹/L (NEU 88%)  
- HGB 131 g/L  
- Platelets 312 ×10⁹/L

**Inflammation**
- CRP 186 mg/L  
- Procalcitonin 7.8 ng/mL

**Renal/Metabolic**
- Glucose 14.2 mmol/L  
- Urea 10.1 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 142 µmol/L (↑ from baseline by history)  
- eGFR 46 mL/min/1.73m²  
- Na 132 mmol/L  
- K 4.6 mmol/L  
- Cl 98 mmol/L  
- Mg 0.72 mmol/L

**Coagulation**
- INR 1.05  
- APTT 27 s

**Cardiac marker**
- hs-TnT 14 ng/L (no dynamic change on repeat at 2 hours: 13 ng/L)

**Microbiology**
- Blood cultures x2 drawn prior to antibiotics  
- Sputum sent for Gram stain/culture  
- Rapid influenza/COVID: negative

---

## Imaging

### Chest X-ray PA/AP (15:05)
Right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits.

**Impression**: Radiographic picture consistent with community-acquired pneumonia (RLL).

---

## Working Diagnoses
1. **Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), right lower lobe**  
2. **Sepsis with hypotension and hyperlactatemia** (suspected respiratory source)  
3. **Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure** (mild–moderate)  
4. **Acute kidney injury** (likely pre-renal in sepsis/dehydration)  
5. COPD (baseline), currently with wheeze

---

## Risk Stratification (documented)
**CURB-65 = 1 (low risk), suitable for outpatient treatment.**  
(Recorded factors: “Age >65 only.”)

---

## Therapy and ED Course

**Initial management**
- Oxygen via nasal cannula 3 L/min → SpO₂ improved to 94–95%  
- IV access x2, blood cultures obtained  
- IV crystalloids: 1,500 mL balanced solution over first hour (with BP response)  
- Antibiotics (after cultures):
  - Ceftriaxone 2 g IV  
  - Azithromycin 500 mg IV  
- Antipyretic: Paracetamol 1 g IV  
- Bronchodilator: Salbutamol/ipratropium nebulization x1  
- Hyperglycemia: capillary glucose monitoring; insulin correction scale initiated  
- Ramipril and SGLT2 inhibitor held due to hypotension/AKI

**Response**
BP improved to 102/66 mmHg, HR downtrending. RR improved but still elevated. Lactate planned for repeat after fluids.

---

## Disposition / Conclusion
Clinical picture consistent with **community-acquired pneumonia complicated by sepsis**, initial hypotension and elevated lactate with oxygen requirement. Plan: **admit to Internal Medicine ward** for IV antibiotics, monitoring, repeat lactate, urine output monitoring, and renal function follow-up. Escalate to ICU if hypotension recurs or oxygen requirement increases.

**Discharge instructions (if later discharged)**: return immediately for worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, persistent fever, or inability to maintain oral intake.

---

Also reviewed medication safety in sepsis/AKI/hypoxia and adequacy of early resuscitation/level-of-care decisions.
</thinking>

<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes  
TYPE: Wrong risk calculation  
QUOTE: "CURB-65 = 1 (low risk), suitable for outpatient treatment."  
PROBLEM: CURB‑65 is miscalculated. This patient meets **RR ≥30 (32/min), urea >7 mmol/L (10.1), BP low (88/56), and age >65**, giving **CURB‑65 = 4 (high risk)**, not 1. Labeling him “low risk/outpatient” could lead to unsafe under-triage, delayed ICU evaluation, and premature discharge in a patient with severe CAP physiology and sepsis.  
ACTION: Recalculate severity correctly (CURB‑65 = 4) and manage as **severe CAP with sepsis**—ensure inpatient admission with high-acuity monitoring and **early ICU/critical care consult** based on hemodynamics/oxygen needs.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
Missing intervention: In sepsis/AKI/hypoxemia, **metformin should also be held** (not just ramipril/SGLT2i) to reduce risk of metformin-associated lactic acidosis; restart only when renal function and perfusion normalize.  
Missing intervention: Initial fluids were **1,500 mL**; for septic hypotension, guidelines commonly target ~**30 mL/kg early** with frequent reassessment—consider additional bolus(es) and documented perfusion reassessment (MAP, urine output, lactate clearance), and be ready for vasopressors if hypotension persists.  
Missing intervention: Given initial **SpO₂ 89% RA / PaO₂ 7.3 kPa** and RR 32 with sepsis, ensure **clear escalation criteria and early senior/ICU review** (especially if oxygen requirement increases, work of breathing remains high, or lactate rises).  
Missing intervention: **Electrolyte abnormality** (Mg 0.72 mmol/L) in a septic, tachycardic patient receiving bronchodilators—consider magnesium repletion and repeat electrolytes to reduce arrhythmia risk.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: CURB-65 = 1 (low risk), suitable for outpatient treatment.
error_explanation: Based on the documented data, CURB-65 should be higher: Age >=65 (1), RR >=30 (1; RR 32), low BP (1; 88/56), and urea >7 mmol/L (1; urea 10.1). That totals 4, not 1. The report also describes sepsis and hypotension, which contradict a 'low risk/outpatient' classification.
correct_action: Recalculate CURB-65 correctly (score 4) and treat as high-risk CAP: urgent inpatient care with close monitoring (often ICU/HDU consideration), sepsis bundle completion, repeat lactate, and ensure appropriate empiric IV antibiotics and hemodynamic/respiratory support.

<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes  
TYPE: Contraindicated medication  
QUOTE: "**Propranolol 40 mg PO twice daily for 7 days for tachycardia**  ← *prescribed at discharge*"  
PROBLEM: Propranolol is a **non-selective beta-blocker** and is generally **contraindicated in asthma** because it can precipitate **bronchospasm** and **blunt the effect of rescue beta-agonists** (salbutamol) and LABA (formoterol), potentially causing treatment-resistant deterioration after discharge.  
ACTION: Do **not** prescribe non-selective beta-blockers in asthmatics; if rate control is truly necessary, use an alternative approach (often **no treatment** as beta-agonist–related tachycardia is transient; if needed, consider a **cardioselective beta-1 blocker at cautious low dose** only with clear indication and close follow-up).
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
Missing intervention: No objective airflow measurement documented (PEF/FEV₁ pre/post treatment) to assess severity and discharge readiness; obtain and use it to guide disposition and relapse risk.  
Wrong clinical priority: Treating tachycardia pharmacologically at discharge is usually unnecessary here and may mask worsening respiratory status; reassess for causes (beta-agonists, anxiety, dehydration) and allow resolution with observation/fluids instead.  
Missed diagnosis (risk not explicitly assessed): Initial hypoxia (SpO₂ 92% RA) and tachycardia warrant explicit consideration of alternative causes (e.g., PE) if any risk factors/history emerge; document structured assessment (PERC/Wells) and test if indicated.  
Drug interaction/therapeutic antagonism: Propranolol would directly oppose prescribed **salbutamol** and **formoterol**, increasing risk of poor bronchodilator response during relapse; remove propranolol and ensure an asthma-appropriate plan.
</other_errors>

<end_token>

error_explanation: Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker that can cause bronchoconstriction and worsen asthma, potentially triggering severe bronchospasm. It is generally contraindicated in patients with asthma, especially immediately after an exacerbation.
correct_action: Do not prescribe non-selective beta-blockers in asthma. Patient reports 2 days of progressive redness, warmth, and tenderness of the left shin with subjective chills. This morning measured temperature 38.5 °C at home. Denies chest pain, dyspnea, cough, abdominal pain, vomiting. No trauma. Notes a small “crack” between toes after new shoes last week.

gold_response:
<thinking>
Key safety-relevant issues: (1) major interaction between TMP‑SMX and warfarin with bleeding risk and need for INR plan, (2) TMP‑SMX monotherapy is suboptimal for typical non‑purulent cellulitis (insufficient streptococcal coverage), (3) TMP can cause hyperkalemia especially with ACE inhibitor/diabetes/older age, (4) portal-of-entry (interdigital tinea/fissure) needs active treatment to prevent recurrence.
</thinking>

<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes  
TYPE: Drug interaction  
QUOTE: "**Trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole (TMP‑SMX) 160/800 mg PO, 1 tablet BID for 7 days**" + "Continue home medications as usual."  
PROBLEM: TMP‑SMX markedly potentiates warfarin (CYP2C9 inhibition and gut flora effects), commonly causing rapid INR elevation and major bleeding; discharging on TMP‑SMX while instructing to continue warfarin “as usual” without an urgent INR monitoring/warfarin dose adjustment plan can directly harm the patient.  
ACTION: Avoid TMP‑SMX when feasible; if it must be used, arrange INR recheck within ~48–72 hours (and again after completion), proactively adjust/hold warfarin per protocol, counsel on bleeding signs, and coordinate with anticoagulation service/PCP the same day.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
- Wrong treatment choice: TMP‑SMX monotherapy for an apparently non‑purulent cellulitis may miss streptococcal coverage; use a beta‑lactam (e.g., cephalexin/amoxicillin-clavulanate) and add MRSA coverage only if risk factors/purulence or use an agent covering both per local guidelines.  
- Missing intervention: No documented explicit anticoagulation safety plan (INR follow-up timing, warfarin dose guidance, bleeding precautions) despite starting a high‑interaction antibiotic; add a clear monitoring and dose-adjustment plan at discharge.  
- Drug interaction/adverse effect risk: TMP can cause hyperkalemia/AKI, especially in older adults on ACE inhibitors (ramipril) and with diabetes; consider alternative antibiotic or plan potassium/creatinine check within a few days and counsel about weakness/palpitations.  
- Missing intervention: Interdigital fissure/maceration likely tinea pedis/portal of entry; “hygiene discussed” alone may be insufficient—prescribe topical antifungal (e.g., terbinafine/clotrimazole) and specific drying/skin-barrier measures to reduce recurrence.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: "Trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole (TMP‑SMX) 160/800 mg PO, 1 tablet BID for 7 days" (given while patient is on warfarin with INR 2.6)
error_explanation: TMP-SMX strongly inhibits warfarin metabolism (notably via CYP2C9) and can markedly increase INR, raising the risk of serious bleeding. The report prescribes TMP-SMX without recognizing/mitigating this dangerous interaction or arranging urgent INR monitoring and dose adjustment.
correct_action: Avoid TMP-SMX in a warfarin-treated patient when possible (choose an alternative antibiotic appropriate for cellulitis and local resistance patterns). If TMP-SMX is necessary, document the interaction, coordinate warfarin dose adjustment, and arrange INR recheck within ~24–72 hours (and again during therapy), with clear bleeding precautions.

TYPE: Missing intervention  
QUOTE: "**Patient discharged home with oral antibiotics (amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125 mg q12h for 7 days), advised to arrange outpatient surgical clinic follow-up within 48–72 hours.**"

ACTION: Cancel discharge and admit under surgery for urgent in-person surgical assessment and likely appendectomy (or inpatient, protocolized IV antibiotics/observation if non-operative pathway is chosen), with ongoing monitoring and reassessment.

- Wrong treatment strategy: Outpatient oral amoxicillin/clavulanate for 7 days is not an appropriate stand-alone plan for suspected complicated appendicitis/appendicolith (higher failure rate and abscess risk); if antibiotics-only approach is chosen it should be inpatient with appropriate IV regimen and clear criteria for failure.  
- Missing intervention: Inadequate sepsis-risk management/monitoring at disposition—tachycardia, fever, leukocytosis, CRP 86, lactate 2.1 warrant observation with repeat vitals ± repeat lactate and escalation if worsening; discharge prevents timely recognition of deterioration.  
- Drug safety / missing intervention: Metformin management around acute illness/possible AKI and IV contrast is under-addressed (mild dehydration, creatinine 108 with unknown baseline); should explicitly hold metformin during acute illness and consider repeat renal function within 48 hours (especially if any concern for AKI) before resuming.
</other_errors>

Denies pleuritic component and denies fever/cough.

Took one tablet of home nitroglycerin (old prescription from a prior evaluation for “angina”) with partial relief; pain recurred.

### Past medical history
- Arterial hypertension (10+ years)
- Hyperlipidemia
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet + metformin)

### Surgical history

- Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1

### Allergies

### Functions / habits
Normal bowel movements and urination. Alcohol occasional (1–2 beers/week). No illicit drug use.

---

## Status (Physical Examination)
Oriented, anxious due to pain, **afebrile**, skin warm with **mild diaphoresis**, well perfused, no cyanosis. No peripheral lymphadenopathy.

**Neck:** no jugular venous distension.  
**Lungs:** vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no wheezes, no crackles.  
**Heart:** regular rhythm, tachycardic, tones audible, no clear murmurs.  
**Abdomen:** soft, non-tender, no guarding, peristalsis present.  
**Extremities:** no edema, peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric, no calf tenderness.

- **ST-segment depression 1–2 mm in V4–V6 and II, aVF**  
- T-wave inversion in V5–V6  
**Interpretation:** ischemic changes consistent with **NSTE-ACS**.

### Radiographic imaging of heart and lungs (portable AP)
**Findings/Opinion:**  
No focal consolidation or pleural effusion. Mild chronic bronchovascular markings. Cardiomediastinal silhouette within upper limits.  
(Dr. M. Radić, 06.02.2026 14:12)

**CBC:**  
WBC 9.8 [1e9]/L; RBC 4.62 [1e12]/L; HGB 142 g/L; Hct 0.418 L/L; MCV 90.5 fL; Plt 221 [1e9]/L.

---

## Clinical Course and Assessment
Findings consistent with **NSTEMI / NSTE-ACS**: typical anginal pain, ischemic ECG changes, and **rising hs-troponin**. Multiple risk factors (HTN, DM2, HLD, former smoker). Requires cardiology evaluation, monitoring, and guideline-based inpatient management.

---

**Discharge instructions:** rest, avoid exertion, strict return precautions for chest pain, dyspnea, syncope.

## Diagnoses
- N20.0 Calculus of kidney (renal colic), suspected left-sided
- R11.2 Nausea with vomiting, unspecified
- R31.9 Hematuria, unspecified (microscopic)

---

## Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 168/96 mmHg; Pulse: 104/min; SpO₂: 98%; Temp: 36.7°C; Resp: 18/min; VAS: 8 – Severe pain; GCS: 15

| 06.02.2026 11:40 | 148/86 | 82 | 99 | 16 | 36.6 | 2 | 15 |

---

## Reason for Visit
Sudden onset severe left flank pain radiating to the groin, nausea/vomiting.

---

## History (Anamnesis)

### Current Illness
Patient reports abrupt onset of severe, colicky pain in the left flank starting ~2 hours prior to arrival. Pain radiates toward the left lower abdomen and groin. Associated nausea and one episode of non-bloody vomiting. He is restless and unable to find a comfortable position. Denies chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, focal neurological symptoms.

He noticed darker urine this morning but no gross visible blood. Mild dysuria without frequency/urgency. No fever or chills.

No recent trauma. No recent travel.

### Past Medical History
- Hypertension (well controlled)
- Hyperlipidemia
- Prior renal stone episode ~6 years ago (passed spontaneously)

### Past Surgical History
- Appendectomy (childhood)

### Medications
- Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1
- Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1

### Allergies
- No known drug allergies

### Social History
- Non-smoker
- Alcohol occasionally
- Works as an accountant

---

## Physical Examination (Status)
General: Moderate build, anxious/restless due to pain, alert and oriented, afebrile, well perfused.

Head/Neck: No meningism. Mucosa slightly dry.

Lungs: Vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no wheeze/crackles. SpO₂ stable on room air.

Heart: Regular rhythm, tachycardic initially, no murmurs.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild tenderness in left lower quadrant without guarding or rebound. No palpable masses. Normal bowel sounds.

Flanks/Kidneys: Marked left costovertebral angle tenderness. Right side non-tender.

Extremities: No edema, pulses palpable, no calf tenderness.

Neurologic: Grossly intact.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations

### ECG
Sinus tachycardia 102/min initially. No ischemic ST-T changes.

### Laboratory Tests (06.02.2026 09:35)

**Hematology – Blood Count**
- WBC 10.8 ×10⁹/L  
- RBC 4.89 ×10¹²/L  
- Hb 148 g/L  
- Hct 0.435 L/L  
- MCV 89 fL  
- Platelets 248 ×10⁹/L  

**Differential**
- Neutrophils 76% (Abs 8.2 ×10⁹/L)  
- Lymphocytes 16% (Abs 1.7 ×10⁹/L)  
- Monocytes 7% (Abs 0.8 ×10⁹/L)  
- Eosinophils 1%  
- Basophils 0%  

**Biochemistry**
- Glucose 6.2 mmol/L  
- Urea 6.4 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 92 µmol/L (baseline unknown)  
- eGFR 86 mL/min/1.73 m²  
- AST 19 U/L, ALT 21 U/L, ALP 74 U/L, GGT 18 U/L  
- Total bilirubin 9 µmol/L  

**Inflammation**
- CRP 4.2 mg/L

**Electrolytes**
- Na 140 mmol/L  
- K 4.1 mmol/L  
- Cl 105 mmol/L  
- Ca 2.29 mmol/L

**Urinalysis (midstream, 06.02.2026 10:00)**
- Appearance: slightly cloudy, yellow
- Specific gravity 1.020
- pH 6.0
- Protein: negative
- Glucose: negative
- Ketones: negative
- Nitrites: negative
- Leukocyte esterase: negative
- Blood: 3+
- Microscopy: RBC 45–60 /HPF, WBC 0–2 /HPF, rare epithelial cells, no casts, no bacteria seen

Interpretation: Microscopic hematuria without evidence of urinary tract infection.

---

## Imaging

### Ultrasound of Kidneys and Bladder (POCUS + formal US, 06.02.2026 10:35)
- Left kidney: mild hydronephrosis (grade I–II), no definite stone visualized (limited by bowel gas).
- Right kidney: no hydronephrosis.
- Bladder: partially filled; no mass seen.

Impression: Findings compatible with left-sided obstructive uropathy, likely ureterolithiasis.

### Non-contrast CT KUB (06.02.2026 11:15)
- 4 mm hyperdense calculus in the distal left ureter approximately 2 cm proximal to the ureterovesical junction.
- Mild left hydroureteronephrosis.
- No perinephric collection, no signs of pyelonephritis.
- No right-sided stones. No other acute abdominal pathology.

Radiology conclusion: Distal left ureteral stone 4 mm with mild upstream dilatation.

---

## Therapy in ED
- IV access, monitoring
- 0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV (bolus then maintenance)
- Ketorolac 30 mg IV once
- Ondansetron 4 mg IV once
- After pain control: oral hydration tolerated

Response: Pain decreased from VAS 8 to VAS 2–3 within ~45 minutes; nausea resolved. Hemodynamically stable, afebrile throughout.

---

## Discharge Plan / Conclusion
Clinical presentation, urinalysis (hematuria without pyuria), ultrasound (mild hydronephrosis), and non-contrast CT confirm **renal colic due to a 4 mm distal left ureteral stone**. No signs of sepsis, acute kidney injury, or obstructing infected stone. No indication for urgent admission.

### Discharge Medications
- Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain (take with food; avoid if gastric intolerance)
- Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO once daily for 14 days (medical expulsive therapy)
- Ondansetron 4 mg PO as needed for nausea (up to 2–3×/day)

### Instructions
- Hydration as tolerated; strain urine and keep any passed stone for analysis.
- Follow-up with urology within 1–2 weeks (earlier if persistent pain).
- Repeat renal function and ultrasound/CT per urology if stone not passed.
- Return to ED immediately for: fever/chills, persistent vomiting/inability to drink, worsening or uncontrolled pain, reduced urine output, fainting, or any new severe symptoms.

Patient discharged home in improved condition, pain controlled, stable vital signs.

---

**Severity/Risk Notes:** CURB-65 score likely ≥2 (age ≥65, urea elevated; plus hypotension borderline), ongoing O₂ requirement and AKI.

<thinking>
Key safety issues: patient meets sepsis criteria with elevated lactate, hypotension, AKI, and ongoing oxygen requirement from hypoxemic respiratory failure. Pneumonia severity scores (CURB-65) indicate high risk and need for inpatient care/monitoring. ED sepsis bundle elements (adequate fluid resuscitation, repeat lactate, close hemodynamic/oxygen monitoring, escalation if unstable) are incomplete for safe discharge. <primary_error>
FOUND: Yes  
TYPE: Missing intervention  
QUOTE: "Patient improved after fluids and first dose of antibiotics; discharged home from ED with oral amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125 mg q12h for 7 days and azithromycin 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg days 2–5."  
PROBLEM: Discharging a 67-year-old with community-acquired pneumonia plus **sepsis physiology (lactate 3.1, tachycardia, borderline hypotension), AKI (Cr 162/eGFR 39), and persistent oxygen requirement (SpO₂ 92% on 2 L NC)** creates high risk of rapid deterioration, missed septic shock progression, and respiratory failure without monitoring. CURB-65 is effectively **3** (Age ≥65, Urea elevated, Diastolic BP ≤60), which generally warrants inpatient management.  
ACTION: Admit (at least monitored bed; consider step-down/ICU depending on response), continue IV antibiotics/oxygen, complete sepsis resuscitation with reassessment and repeat lactate, and monitor renal/respiratory status.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
- Missing intervention: Sepsis management appears incomplete for this presentation (only 1 L fluid given despite hypotension/CRT ~3 s and lactate 3.1; no documented repeat lactate or formal post-fluid perfusion reassessment/vasopressor consideration if hypotension persists).  
- Missing intervention: Failure to address medication safety in AKI/sepsis—no instruction to **hold metformin** (risk of lactic acidosis in acute illness/renal impairment) and typically **hold ramipril** during AKI/hypotension until renal function and hemodynamics recover.  
- Wrong risk calculation: CURB-65 severity is understated (“likely ≥2”); based on recorded values it is **3**, supporting admission and closer monitoring rather than outpatient therapy.  
- Missing intervention: Possible parapneumonic effusion noted on CXR (“small parapneumonic effusion cannot be excluded”) without a plan for follow-up imaging/ultrasound if clinical course worsens—could miss evolving complicated effusion/empyema.
</other_errors>

Discharging risks deterioration, respiratory failure, and progression of sepsis without monitoring, IV therapy, and reassessment.
correct_action: Admit to hospital (medical ward or higher level if instability persists) for oxygen therapy, IV antibiotics with monitoring, repeat lactate and labs, fluid management, urine output monitoring, and reassessment of hemodynamics/respiratory status. Consider severity scoring (CURB-65/PSI), evaluate for complications (effusion, bacteremia), and ensure clinical stability before any discharge.

06.02.2026 10:05 | 132/84 | 88 | 99 | 16 | 36.6 | 3 | 15

---

## Reason for Visit
Acute right-sided flank pain

---

## History
**CURRENT ILLNESS:** Patient reports sudden onset of severe right flank pain starting ~2 hours prior to arrival, described as colicky, waxing/waning, radiating toward the right groin. Associated nausea without vomiting. He notes urinary urgency and mild dysuria since this morning. Denies fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, or trauma. No testicular swelling; pain is not positional and does not worsen with respiration.

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:** Prior kidney stone ~5 years ago (passed spontaneously). No known diabetes. No known chronic kidney disease.

**MEDICATIONS:** None regularly.

**ALLERGIES:** No known drug allergies.

**SOCIAL HISTORY:** Occasional alcohol; non-smoker.

**FUNCTIONS:** Last bowel movement yesterday, normal. No gross blood in stool. Urination today frequent in small amounts.

---

## Status (Physical Examination)
Medium build, in visible discomfort, intermittently restless due to pain. Oriented x3, GCS 15. Skin warm, well perfused, no rash.

- **HEENT:** Mucosa moist, no icterus.
- **Chest/Lungs:** Symmetric chest movement; clear breath sounds bilaterally.
- **Heart:** Regular rhythm, no murmurs; peripheral pulses palpable.
- **Abdomen:** Soft, non-distended. Mild right lower quadrant tenderness without rebound/guarding. No palpable masses.  
- **CVA tenderness:** Marked tenderness over right costovertebral angle; left side non-tender.
- **GU:** No scrotal swelling or focal testicular tenderness reported; exam deferred by patient, no red-flag symptoms suggesting torsion.
- **Extremities:** No edema, no focal neurologic deficit.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations

### ECG
Sinus tachycardia ~102/min. No ischemic ST-T changes.

### Laboratory Results

**HEMATOLOGY – BLOOD COUNT:**  
WBC 10.9 [10⁹/L]; RBC 5.02 [10¹²/L]; Hb 151 g/L; Hct 0.447 L/L; MCV 89.0 fL; MCH 30.1 pg; MCHC 338 g/L; RDW-CV 13.1%; Platelets 242 [10⁹/L]; MPV 10.2 fL

**HEMATOLOGY – DIFFERENTIAL:**  
NEU% 78.0%; LYM% 15.3%; MONO% 5.8%; EOS% 0.6%; BASO% 0.3%  
NEU # 8.50 [10⁹/L]; LYM # 1.67 [10⁹/L]; MONO # 0.63 [10⁹/L]; EOS # 0.07 [10⁹/L]; BASO # 0.03 [10⁹/L]

**METABOLITES AND SUBSTRATES:**  
Glucose 5.6 mmol/L; Urea 6.4 mmol/L; Creatinine 86 µmol/L; eGFR 109 mL/min/1.73m²

**INFLAMMATION / PROTEINS:**  
CRP 3.2 mg/L

**ELECTROLYTES:**  
Na 140 mmol/L; K 4.3 mmol/L; Cl 104 mmol/L; Ca 2.30 mmol/L

**URINALYSIS (dipstick + sediment):**  
- Color: yellow; clarity: slightly cloudy  
- Specific gravity 1.020; pH 6.0  
- Protein: trace; Glucose: negative; Ketones: negative  
- Nitrite: negative; Leukocyte esterase: negative  
- Blood: 3+  
**Microscopy:** RBC 50–100 /HPF; WBC 0–3 /HPF; bacteria not seen; crystals: occasional calcium oxalate  
(Findings consistent with hematuria without evidence of urinary tract infection.)

### Imaging
**Non-contrast CT KUB (kidneys/ureters/bladder):**  
- 4 mm calculus in the **right distal ureter** near the ureterovesical junction.  
- Mild right hydroureter and mild hydronephrosis.  
- No perinephric stranding.  
- No additional stones causing obstruction.  
**Impression:** Right distal ureterolithiasis with mild upstream dilatation.

---

## Therapy in ED
- IV access, monitoring.
- **Analgesia:** Ketorolac 30 mg IV once.  
- **Antiemetic:** Ondansetron 4 mg IV once.  
- **Hydration:** 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV.

**Clinical response:** Pain decreased from VAS 8/10 to 3/10 within ~45 minutes; nausea resolved. Hemodynamically stable; afebrile.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture, urinalysis, and CT are consistent with **right distal ureteral stone (4 mm)** with mild hydronephrosis. - **Return immediately / call EMS if:** fever ≥ 38°C, rigors, worsening flank pain not controlled with medication, inability to urinate, persistent vomiting/dehydration, fainting, or any new severe symptoms.

- hs‑troponin I is markedly elevated and rising (186 → 214 ng/L) → acute myocardial injury until proven otherwise (NSTEMI/unstable angina vs other causes).

error_explanation: This conclusion ignores critical cardiac biomarker results: hs-Troponin I is markedly elevated (186 ng/L) with a rising repeat value (214 ng/L). A rising troponin pattern indicates myocardial injury and is concerning for acute coronary syndrome (NSTEMI) even if ECG is nondiagnostic and pain is reproducible. Discharging the patient without addressing elevated/rising troponin is unsafe.
correct_action: Treat as suspected ACS/NSTEMI: do not discharge; initiate ACS protocol (aspirin unless contraindicated, consider anticoagulation/antianginal therapy per local guidelines), obtain serial ECGs and troponins, calculate risk (e.g., HEART score), consult cardiology, and admit for telemetry and further evaluation (possible coronary angiography/stress testing depending on pathway). Also address risk factors and ensure strict return precautions.

report_content:
MBOO: 284915607

Date of Birth: 1946

---

## Emergency Department Report

### Triage Category: 3

### Chief Complaint
Fever with chills and right-sided flank pain for 2 days, dysuria and urinary frequency.

### Diagnoses
- N10 Acute pyelonephritis  
- R50.9 Fever, unspecified  
- E86.0 Dehydration  

---

## Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 104/66 mmHg; Pulse: 112/min; SpO2: 96%; Temp: 38.7°C; VAS: 7/10; GCS: 15;

---

## Anamnesis

### Past Medical History
Atrial fibrillation (on chronic anticoagulation), arterial hypertension, hyperlipidemia. No known chronic kidney disease. No prior urologic procedures.

### Medications (home)
- Warfarin (variable dosing per INR card; last INR control ~10 days ago per patient)
- Bisoprolol 2.5 mg 1×1  
- Ramipril 5 mg 1×1  
- Simvastatin 20 mg 1×0×1  

### Allergies
No known drug allergies.

### Current Illness
Started with dysuria and urinary frequency 3 days ago; developed fever (up to 39°C) and right flank pain with nausea over the last 48 hours. No vomiting today. Denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or syncope.

### Functions / Habits
Reduced oral intake today. Normal bowel movements. Non-smoker, rare alcohol.

---

## Status (Physical Examination)
Elderly female, oriented, appears ill and febrile, mildly dehydrated (dry mucosa).  
Skin warm, no rash. No peripheral edema.

**Lungs:** Vesicular breathing, no crackles/wheezing.  
**Heart:** Irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic; no murmurs.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness.  
**Kidneys:** Marked right costovertebral angle tenderness.  
**Neurologic:** No focal deficit.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG
Atrial fibrillation with ventricular response ~110/min, no acute ischemic changes.

### Laboratory Tests
- WBC 14.8 [1e9]/L (NEU 86%)  
- RBC 4.22 [1e12]/L, HGB 126 g/L, Hct 0.383 L/L  
- Platelets 214 [1e9]/L  
- CRP 126 mg/L  
- Glucose 6.7 mmol/L  
- Urea 9.8 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 112 µmol/L, eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m²  
- Na 133 mmol/L, K 4.3 mmol/L, Cl 99 mmol/L  
- AST 28 U/L, ALT 22 U/L  
- Lactate 1.6 mmol/L  
- INR 2.6, APTT 33 s  

**Urinalysis (dip + sediment):**  
Appearance: cloudy; Nitrites: positive; Leukocytes: +++; Protein: +; Blood: ++.  
Sediment: WBC “numerous”, RBC 10–15/HPF, bacteria “many”.

(Blood and urine cultures obtained prior to antibiotics.)

### Imaging

**Ultrasound of kidneys and urinary tract (bedside):**  
Both kidneys visualized, no hydronephrosis, no visible obstructing stone, bladder moderately filled. Findings compatible with uncomplicated upper UTI; correlate clinically.

---

## Therapy in ED
- 1000 mL 0.9% NaCl IV (over ~90 minutes)  
- Paracetamol 1 g IV  
- Ondansetron 4 mg IV  
- Ceftriaxone 2 g IV (after cultures)  
- Analgesia: Metamizole 1 g IV (single dose)

Re-evaluation after treatment: Temp 37.9°C, BP 112/70 mmHg, pulse 96/min; pain decreased to VAS 3/10; tolerating oral fluids.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture and labs consistent with **acute right-sided pyelonephritis** without sonographic obstruction. After IV fluids/antipyretic/first-dose antibiotic, patient hemodynamically stable, improved, able to take oral intake.

**Discharge plan:**
- Start **trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole (TMP/SMX) 160/800 mg, 1 tablet PO twice daily for 10 days**.
- Encourage oral hydration.
- Paracetamol 500–1000 mg PO as needed for fever/pain (max 3 g/day).
- Follow up with GP in 48–72 hours; return immediately if worsening pain, persistent fever >48 h, vomiting/inability to hydrate, confusion, or hypotension.
- Await culture results; antibiotic may be adjusted by GP/ED if resistant organism.

---
gold_response:
<thinking>
- Assessed acuity/risk: elderly patient with febrile pyelonephritis, initial tachycardia and borderline BP, mild AKI/dehydration (eGFR 48).
- Reviewed treatment choices for pyelonephritis (initial IV ceftriaxone appropriate), then evaluated discharge oral antibiotic choice and comorbid medication risks.
- Screened for high-risk medication interactions (warfarin, ACE inhibitor) and for need for admission/close monitoring in older adults with possible complicated infection.
</thinking>

<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes  
TYPE: Drug interaction  
QUOTE: "Start **trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole (TMP/SMX) 160/800 mg, 1 tablet PO twice daily for 10 days**."  
PROBLEM: TMP/SMX has a strong, well-known interaction with **warfarin** (CYP2C9 inhibition and reduced vitamin K–producing flora), often causing **marked INR elevation and major bleeding** risk—especially in older adults and with baseline INR already therapeutic-high (INR 2.6). The plan does not include urgent INR monitoring or warfarin dose adjustment, creating avoidable harm risk.  
ACTION: Avoid TMP/SMX in a warfarin-treated patient when alternatives exist, or if used, **arrange INR check within 48–72 hours (often sooner), proactively adjust/hold warfarin per protocol, and provide explicit bleeding precautions**.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
- Missing intervention: No explicit plan to monitor/mitigate **TMP/SMX + ramipril**–associated hyperkalemia/renal function decline (needs K/creatinine recheck within a few days, or choose alternative antibiotic).  
- Wrong antibiotic strategy: **Empiric** TMP/SMX for pyelonephritis is suboptimal unless the organism is known susceptible / local resistance is low; an oral regimen with more reliable empiric activity (guided by local antibiogram) or continued parenteral/observational management may be safer until cultures return.  
- Missed diagnosis/risk stratification: This presentation in a **79-year-old** with initial tachycardia, borderline BP, dehydration and reduced eGFR may represent **complicated pyelonephritis/early sepsis risk**; discharge may be unsafe without observation/admission criteria review and tighter follow-up (e.g., next-day reassessment).  
- Missing intervention: No documented plan for **early culture-result follow-up** (same/next day callback system) despite discharge with a potentially high-risk antibiotic choice and possibility of resistance or deterioration.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: Start trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole (TMP/SMX) 160/800 mg, 1 tablet PO twice daily for 10 days.
error_explanation: The patient is taking warfarin with INR 2.6. TMP/SMX strongly increases warfarin effect (CYP2C9 inhibition and reduced vitamin K–producing gut flora), significantly raising INR and bleeding risk. The report fails to recognize and manage this dangerous interaction.
correct_action: Avoid TMP/SMX in a warfarin-treated patient when possible (choose an alternative guided by local resistance/cultures, e.g., cephalosporin or amoxicillin/clavulanate if appropriate). If TMP/SMX is necessary, preemptively reduce warfarin dose and arrange urgent INR monitoring within 2–3 days and again during therapy, with clear bleeding precautions.

report_content:
**EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT REPORT (NEW CASE)**

Surname and Name: **KOVAČEVIĆ MARIN**  
Patient ID (MBOO): **204918736**  
Address: **Ulica Domovinskog rata 42, Split**  
Date of Birth: **17.08.1987** (Age: 36)  
Date of Arrival: **06.02.2026, 19:22**  
Date of Discharge: **06.02.2026, 22:05**  
Triage Category: **2 (Very urgent)**

---

## Diagnoses
- **T78.2 Anaphylactic reaction, unspecified**
- **L50.0 Allergic urticaria**

---

## Triage Data (19:26)
- **BP:** 88/56 mmHg  
- **Pulse:** 124/min  
- **SpO₂:** 93% (room air)  
- **Resp:** 24/min  
- **Temp:** 36.7 °C  
- **GCS:** 15  
- **VAS:** 3/10 (throat tightness/discomfort)

Chief complaint at triage: **Generalized rash, lip swelling, throat tightness, shortness of breath after food ingestion**

---

## History (Anamnesis)
**Present illness:**  
Patient reports sudden onset of diffuse itching and hives followed by **lip swelling** and **throat tightness** ~20 minutes after eating a restaurant meal (suspected **shellfish** in sauce). Developed mild wheezing and shortness of breath while walking to the car. No syncope, no chest pain. EMS not involved; arrived by family.

**Associated symptoms:** generalized urticaria, facial/lip edema, hoarse voice (“slightly”), dyspnea, nausea without vomiting. Denies fever.

**Past medical history:** seasonal allergic rhinitis; no prior anaphylaxis. No asthma diagnosis.

**Medications:** cetirizine PRN (rare use).

**Allergies:** denies known drug allergies; **possible food allergy** not previously confirmed.

**Family history:** non-contributory for atopy/anaphylaxis.

**Habits:** occasional alcohol; non-smoker.

**Functions:** normal urination; normal stool.

---

## Physical Examination
**General:** anxious, oriented, speaks in full sentences but with intermittent throat clearing; afebrile; skin warm and well perfused.

**Skin:** diffuse urticarial wheals on trunk and extremities; excoriations from scratching.

**Head/neck:** mild **lip edema**; mild perioral edema; oropharynx without visible uvular deviation; **no drooling**, mild hoarseness reported; no stridor at rest.

**Chest:** symmetrical expansion.

**Lungs:** bilateral expiratory wheeze, more pronounced basally; no crackles.  
- **SpO₂ 93% RA** initially.

**Cardiovascular:** tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Peripheral pulses palpable; capillary refill ~2–3 sec.

**Abdomen:** soft, non-tender, no guarding; normal bowel sounds.

**Extremities:** no edema; no calf tenderness.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
### ECG (19:40)
- Sinus tachycardia 122/min  
- No ST elevation/depression; QTc within normal limits

### Laboratory results (20:05)
**CBC:**
- WBC 12.1 ×10⁹/L  
- RBC 4.88 ×10¹²/L  
- Hemoglobin 148 g/L  
- Hematocrit 0.439 L/L  
- Platelets 246 ×10⁹/L  
- Neutrophils 78% (9.4 ×10⁹/L)  
- Lymphocytes 15% (1.8 ×10⁹/L)  
- Eosinophils 1.2% (0.15 ×10⁹/L)

**Biochemistry:**
- Glucose 7.2 mmol/L  
- Urea 6.1 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 86 µmol/L (eGFR 98 mL/min/1.73 m²)  
- AST 18 U/L, ALT 16 U/L, GGT 22 U/L  
- CRP 3.8 mg/L

**Electrolytes:**
- Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, Cl 104 mmol/L, Ca 2.33 mmol/L, Mg 0.78 mmol/L

### Venous blood gas (20:10)
- pH 7.36  
- pCO₂ 44 mmHg  
- HCO₃⁻ 24 mmol/L  
- Lactate 2.1 mmol/L

### Imaging
**Chest X-ray:** not performed (clinical picture consistent with allergic reaction; no focal lung findings suggesting pneumonia).

---

## Therapy in ED
- Oxygen via nasal cannula **3 L/min** (SpO₂ improved to 97%)  
- **0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV** (bolus) due to hypotension  
- **Chloropyramine (Synopen) 20 mg IV**  
- **Methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol) 125 mg IV**  
- **Salbutamol nebulization 2.5 mg** (x2 over observation)  
- Observation with serial vitals

**Control vitals:**
- 20:10 BP 96/61, pulse 108, SpO₂ 97% on 3 L O₂  
- 21:15 BP 110/70, pulse 92, SpO₂ 97% on room air; wheeze minimal  
- 21:50 urticaria regressing; lip edema markedly reduced; patient reports throat tightness resolved

---

## Course / Medical Decision Making
Clinical picture consistent with **anaphylaxis** (skin involvement + respiratory symptoms + hypotension) temporally related to suspected food allergen. Patient improved during ED observation with IV fluids, antihistamine, corticosteroid, bronchodilator, and oxygen.

> **Documented plan:** “Given the clinical improvement, there is no need for adrenaline at this time.”

---

## Disposition / Conclusion
Patient clinically stable after observation: hemodynamically compensated, no dyspnea, no stridor, SpO₂ stable on room air, rash regressing.

**Discharged home** with instructions:
- Avoid suspected trigger foods (shellfish and sauces of unknown content) until evaluation.
- **Cetirizine 10 mg** 1x1 for 5 days; may increase to 2x1 if needed.
- **Prednisone 40 mg** daily for 3 days.
- Arrange outpatient **allergology** evaluation.
- Return immediately / call EMS for recurrence of throat swelling, breathing difficulty, dizziness, or generalized rash.

---
gold_response:
<thinking>
Reviewed presentation against anaphylaxis criteria (skin + respiratory compromise + hypotension after likely food exposure) and compared to ED interventions, monitoring duration, and discharge safety measures.
</thinking>

<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes  
TYPE: Missing intervention  
QUOTE: "Given the clinical improvement, there is no need for adrenaline at this time."  
PROBLEM: The patient met clear criteria for anaphylaxis at arrival (generalized urticaria + wheeze/hoarseness/throat tightness + hypotension BP 88/56) and should have received immediate IM epinephrine as first-line therapy. Withholding epinephrine in anaphylaxis increases risk of airway compromise, refractory shock, biphasic reaction, and death; improvement after fluids/adjuncts does not negate the initial indication.  
ACTION: Administer IM epinephrine promptly (adult 0.3–0.5 mg of 1 mg/mL to lateral thigh), repeat every 5–15 min if needed, and continue resuscitation/airway readiness with close monitoring.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
- Missing intervention: Discharged without an epinephrine auto-injector prescription/training despite food-triggered anaphylaxis; provide 2 auto-injectors, written action plan, and clear instruction to use immediately with recurrence.  
- Missing intervention: Observation period likely too short for a case with initial hypotension/respiratory symptoms (arrival 19:22, discharge 22:05); higher-risk anaphylaxis typically warrants prolonged observation (often ≥6 hours, longer if severe/protracted) or admission if instability/recurrence risk.  
- Missing intervention: Initial treatment sequence prioritized antihistamine/steroid/neb and fluids without epinephrine; these are adjuncts and should not delay first-line IM epinephrine in anaphylaxis.  
- Contraindicated medication (risk-based): IV first-generation antihistamine (chloropyramine) can contribute to sedation and hypotension; if used, give only after epinephrine/hemodynamic stabilization and monitor closely.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: “Given the clinical improvement, there is no need for adrenaline at this time.”
error_explanation: The presentation meets anaphylaxis criteria (urticaria + respiratory symptoms + hypotension). First-line treatment is immediate intramuscular epinephrine; delaying/omitting it increases risk of deterioration or biphasic reaction. Antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts and do not treat airway edema or shock effectively.
correct_action: Administer IM epinephrine (adult: 0.3–0.5 mg of 1 mg/mL solution into the anterolateral thigh), repeat every 5–15 minutes if needed; continue airway/oxygen support, IV fluids, monitoring, and prescribe an epinephrine auto-injector with education and appropriate observation period.

**Emergency Department Report (NEW CASE)**

**Patient:** Female, 36 years  
**Date of birth:** 1989-11-03  
**Visit date/time:** 2026-02-06, 18:22  
**Arrival:** Ambulance (EMS)  
**Triage category:** 2

---

## Diagnoses (ED)
- **T78.2** Anaphylactic shock, unspecified  
- **L50.0** Allergic urticaria  
- **J45.909** Asthma, unspecified, uncomplicated (history)

---

## Triage Data
**Chief complaint:** Sudden generalized rash, lip swelling, shortness of breath after eating.

**Triage vitals (18:26):**  
- **BP:** 88/54 mmHg  
- **Pulse:** 124 /min  
- **SpO₂:** 92% (room air)  
- **Resp:** 24 /min  
- **Temp:** 36.7 °C  
- **VAS:** 2/10 (itching/discomfort)  
- **GCS:** 15  
- **AVPU:** Alert

---

## Vital Signs (ED course)

| Date/Time | BP (mmHg) | Pulse (/min) | SpO₂ (%) | Resp (/min) | Temp (°C) | VAS | GCS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 18:26 | 88/54 | 124 | 92 RA | 24 | 36.7 | 2 | 15 |
| 18:40 | 94/58 | 118 | 96 (O₂ 4 L/min NC) | 22 | 36.7 | 2 | 15 |
| 19:10 | 106/66 | 102 | 97 (O₂ 2 L/min NC) | 18 | 36.8 | 1 | 15 |
| 20:05 | 112/70 | 92 | 98 RA | 16 | 36.8 | 0 | 15 |

---

## Reason for Visit
Allergic reaction with dyspnea after food exposure.

---

## History
**CURRENT ILLNESS:** Patient reports eating at a restaurant ~20 minutes prior to symptom onset (suspected cross-contamination with nuts). Developed itching of palms, diffuse hives, swelling of lips, throat “tightness,” and wheezing. EMS noted generalized urticaria and hypotension. No syncope. No vomiting or diarrhea.  

**Past medical history:** Mild intermittent asthma (uses salbutamol PRN). No cardiac history.  

**Medications:** Salbutamol inhaler PRN; combined oral contraceptive.  

**Allergies:** **Peanuts/tree nuts (known)**. No drug allergies reported.  

**Social:** Non-smoker, occasional alcohol.  

**Gyne/ob:** Not pregnant per history; LMP 2 weeks ago.

---

## Physical Examination (on arrival)
**General:** Anxious, speaking in short sentences initially.  
**Skin:** Diffuse urticaria on trunk/arms; facial flushing; mild lip edema.  
**Airway:** No stridor; reports throat tightness; uvula mildly edematous.  
**Breathing:** Bilateral expiratory wheeze; increased work of breathing initially.  
**Circulation:** Cool peripheries initially; cap refill ~3 seconds; tachycardic.  
**Heart:** Regular tachycardia, no murmurs.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender.  
**Neuro:** GCS 15, no focal deficit.

---

## Diagnostics and Consultations

### ECG (18:33)
Sinus tachycardia ~120/min. No ischemic ST-T changes.

### Imaging
**Chest X-ray (portable AP, 19:02):**  
- No focal consolidation, no pleural effusion, no pneumothorax.  
- Mild peribronchial thickening consistent with reactive airways.  
**Impression:** No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality.

### Laboratory Results (18:45)
**Hematology – CBC:**  
- WBC 10.8 ×10⁹/L  
- Hb 138 g/L  
- Hct 0.409 L/L  
- Platelets 244 ×10⁹/L

**Differential:**  
- Neut 72% (Abs 7.8) ×10⁹/L  
- Lymph 20% (Abs 2.2) ×10⁹/L  
- Eos 2% (Abs 0.2) ×10⁹/L

**Metabolic panel:**  
- Na 140 mmol/L  
- K 3.9 mmol/L  
- Cl 104 mmol/L  
- CO₂ (bicarb) 22 mmol/L  
- Glucose 6.4 mmol/L  
- Urea 4.8 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 74 µmol/L (eGFR 96 mL/min/1.73m²)

**Inflammation:** CRP 2.1 mg/L

**Venous blood gas (18:48, on O₂ 4 L/min):**  
- pH 7.36  
- pCO₂ 42 mmHg  
- HCO₃⁻ 23 mmol/L  
- Lactate 2.3 mmol/L

---

## Therapy / ED Management
- Oxygen via nasal cannula **4 L/min**, titrated down as SpO₂ improved  
- **0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV bolus**  
- **Salbutamol + ipratropium nebulization** ×2  
- **Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV**  
- **Clemastine 2 mg IV**  
- Observation with serial vitals for ~2 hours

**Clinical response:** Rash decreased, wheeze resolved, throat tightness improved, BP normalized after fluids, saturating well on room air at discharge.

---

## Disposition / Conclusion
Presentation is consistent with **anaphylaxis** (food-triggered) with hypotension and respiratory symptoms. After ED treatment and observation, patient improved clinically with stable vital signs and no ongoing respiratory distress.  

**Discharged home** in good general condition with:  
- Prescription: oral antihistamine for 3 days; prednisone 40 mg daily for 3 days  
- Advised strict nut avoidance and follow-up with allergology within 1–2 weeks  
- Return precautions: any recurrence of wheeze, throat swelling, dizziness, vomiting, or rash

---
gold_response:
<thinking>
Key safety issue: the presentation meets criteria for anaphylaxis with shock, yet first-line epinephrine was not administered; discharge planning also lacks standard anaphylaxis safety measures (auto-injector + adequate observation).
</thinking>

<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes  
TYPE: Missing intervention  
QUOTE: "Therapy / ED Management: ... 0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV bolus ... Salbutamol + ipratropium nebulization ×2 ... Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV ... Clemastine 2 mg IV ... Observation with serial vitals for ~2 hours"  
PROBLEM: This patient had anaphylaxis with hypotension (BP 88/54) and respiratory involvement (wheeze, throat tightness/uvular edema), but there is no administration of IM epinephrine—the time-critical, first-line treatment that reduces risk of airway compromise, refractory shock, and death. Steroids/antihistamines/nebulizers and fluids are adjuncts and should not replace epinephrine.  
ACTION: Give IM epinephrine immediately (adult 0.3–0.5 mg of 1 mg/mL [1:1000] into the anterolateral thigh; repeat every 5–15 min as needed), with escalation to epinephrine infusion/airway management if not rapidly improving.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
Missing intervention: Discharged without an epinephrine auto-injector prescription and documented anaphylaxis action plan/training (high risk of recurrence after discharge).  
Wrong risk calculation: Observation period likely too short for anaphylaxis with shock—"Observation with serial vitals for ~2 hours" (many patients with severe features need longer ED observation/admission due to biphasic/protracted reactions).  
Missing intervention: Triage/disposition should reflect high acuity (initial hypotension + airway symptoms); management should occur in a resuscitation/high-acuity setting with readiness for difficult airway/rapid deterioration.  
Contraindicated medication (relative): Use of sedating IV H1 antihistamine during shock/airway symptoms ("Clemastine 2 mg IV") can worsen hypotension and obscure clinical deterioration; it should only be adjunctive after epinephrine and hemodynamic stabilization.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location:
Therapy / ED Management:
- Oxygen via nasal cannula 4 L/min, titrated down as SpO₂ improved
- 0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV bolus
- Salbutamol + ipratropium nebulization ×2
- Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV
- Clemastine 2 mg IV
- Observation with serial vitals for ~2 hours
error_explanation: The patient met criteria for anaphylaxis (known nut allergy with hypotension and respiratory symptoms). First-line, life-saving treatment is immediate intramuscular epinephrine. Treating only with fluids, bronchodilator, antihistamine, and steroid delays definitive therapy and increases risk of biphasic reaction, airway compromise, and death.
correct_action: Administer IM epinephrine promptly (adult: 0.3–0.5 mg of 1 mg/mL [1:1000] solution into the anterolateral thigh), repeat every 5–15 minutes if needed; continue IV fluids, airway monitoring, and observe for at least 4–6 hours (longer if severe). Prescribe/dispense epinephrine autoinjectors and provide an anaphylaxis action plan.

**EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT REPORT (SYNTHETIC)**

**Surname and Name:** BABIĆ MARKO  
**Patient ID (MBOO):** 184503772

**Date of Arrival:** 06.02.2026, 18:27  
**Date of Birth:** 19.11.1957 (68 years)  
**Date of Discharge:** 06.02.2026, 22:05  
**Triage Category:** 3  

---

## Diagnoses
- **J18.9** Pneumonia, unspecified organism  
- **R06.02** Shortness of breath (symptom)  

---

## Triage Data
**Triage Cat:** 3  
**Blood Pressure:** 146/88 mmHg  
**Pulse:** 104/min  
**SpO₂:** 92% (room air)  
**Resp:** 22/min  
**Temp:** 38.2 °C  
**VAS:** 3/10 (pleuritic chest discomfort with coughing)  
**GCS:** 15  

---

## Reason for Arrival
Fever, productive cough, and shortness of breath for 3 days.

---

## Anamnesis (History)
**Present Illness:**  
Patient reports 3 days of fever and chills, productive cough with yellow sputum, progressive dyspnea on exertion, and mild right-sided pleuritic chest pain when coughing. No syncope. Denies hemoptysis. No recent travel. Wife had “a cold” one week ago.

**Past Medical History:**  
- Hypertension  
- Hyperlipidemia  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet + metformin)  
- No known chronic lung disease

**Medications (home):**  
- Ramipril 5 mg 1×1  
- Metformin 1000 mg 2×1  
- **Simvastatin 40 mg 1×1 (evening)**  
- Acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg 1×1  

**Allergies:** Denies drug allergies.

**Habits:** Former smoker (25 pack-years), quit 6 years ago. Alcohol occasionally.

**Functions:** Reduced appetite. Urination normal. No vomiting/diarrhea.

---

## Physical Examination
**General:** Ill-appearing but cooperative, oriented, speaking in full sentences, mildly tachypneic at rest. Skin warm, well perfused.

**HEENT:** Oropharynx mildly injected, no exudate. No neck stiffness.

**Chest/Lungs:** Chest expansion symmetric. Auscultation: decreased breath sounds and crackles at right lower lung field; no wheeze.  
SpO₂ improves to 95% on 2 L/min nasal cannula.

**Heart:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic. No murmurs.

**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.

**Extremities:** No peripheral edema. Calves soft, non-tender.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG
- Sinus tachycardia ~102/min  
- No acute ST elevation/depression, no dynamic ischemic changes

### Imaging
**Chest X-ray (PA + lateral):**  
- Patchy alveolar opacity/consolidation in **right lower lobe**  
- No pleural effusion  
- Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits  
**Impression:** Findings consistent with right lower lobe pneumonia.

### Laboratory Results (06.02.2026 19:10)
**CBC:**  
- WBC **13.8 ×10⁹/L** (↑)  
- RBC 4.72 ×10¹²/L  
- HGB 141 g/L  
- Hct 0.417 L/L  
- MCV 88.3 fL  
- Platelets 232 ×10⁹/L  
- Neutrophils 82% (absolute 11.3 ×10⁹/L) (↑)  

**Inflammation:**  
- CRP **96 mg/L** (↑)  
- Procalcitonin **0.42 ng/mL** (mildly ↑)

**Chemistry:**  
- Glucose 9.8 mmol/L (↑)  
- Urea 7.2 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 92 µmol/L (eGFR 79 mL/min/1.73m²)  
- Na 137 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, Cl 101 mmol/L  
- AST 24 U/L, ALT 19 U/L, GGT 22 U/L  
- Lactate 1.4 mmol/L  

**Cardiac markers:**  
- hs-Troponin T 7 ng/L (within reference, no rise on repeat at 2h)  

**Microbiology:**  
- SARS‑CoV‑2/Influenza A/B rapid PCR: negative  
- Blood cultures drawn ×2 (pending)

---

## ED Course / Observation
Oxygen via nasal cannula 2 L/min with improvement in SpO₂ to 95–96%. After antipyretic and fluids, patient reports easier breathing. Hemodynamically stable throughout observation. CURB‑65 calculated as 1 (age ≥65), suitable for outpatient treatment with close follow-up given clinical stability and good support at home.

---

## Therapy in ED
- Oxygen 2 L/min via nasal cannula (weaned off before discharge; SpO₂ 94–95% on room air)  
- Paracetamol 1 g PO  
- 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV  
- First antibiotic dose given in ED: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV

---

## Discharge Therapy / Prescriptions
- **Clarithromycin 500 mg PO 2×1 for 7 days**  
- Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125 mg PO 2×1 for 7 days  
- Paracetamol 1 g PO as needed for fever (max 3 g/day)  
- Encourage oral hydration, rest

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture, labs (leukocytosis, elevated CRP), and chest X-ray are consistent with community-acquired right lower lobe pneumonia. No evidence of acute coronary syndrome. Patient improved during ED observation, maintains adequate oxygenation on room air, and is safe for discharge with oral antibiotics and strict return precautions.

**Recommendations:**
- Follow up with family physician within 24–48 hours.
- Return immediately for worsening shortness of breath, persistent fever >48 hours after antibiotics, chest pain at rest, confusion, or SpO₂ <92%.
- Review blood culture results with primary care when available.

**Physician:** Dr. I. Novak  
**Time:** 06.02.2026, 22:05

With **age ≥65 + urea >7**, the score is **2**, which generally supports **inpatient management/short-stay observation** rather than routine discharge; the incorrect score may have contributed to an unsafe disposition.

**General:** Alert, oriented, tired-appearing, febrile, mildly dyspneic at rest, speaking full sentences. Skin warm, adequately perfused.

**HEENT:** Oropharynx mildly erythematous, no exudate. No neck stiffness.

**CBC**

- Platelets 238 [1e9]/L  

**Inflammation**
- CRP **146** mg/L (↑)

- Na 136 mmol/L  
- K 4.1 mmol/L  
- Cl 101 mmol/L  
- Urea 5.8 mmol/L

- Antipyretic: **Paracetamol 1 g PO** (fever and discomfort)  
- Empiric antibiotics for community-acquired pneumonia with penicillin allergy history (non-anaphylactic):  
  - **Ceftriaxone 2 g IV** (administered in ED; tolerated without reaction)  
  - **Azithromycin 500 mg PO** (first dose in ED)

Reassessment after therapy (approx. 2 hours):
- Temp 37.8 °C, HR 98/min, BP 116/72 mmHg

Due to **ongoing oxygen requirement** and systemic features (fever, tachypnea), patient requires **inpatient treatment and observation**.

**Plan:**

- Encourage oral fluids as tolerated; IV fluids as needed based on intake/output and vitals.  
- Consider sputum culture if adequate sample; blood cultures if recurrent fever/clinical worsening.  
- DVT prophylaxis per ward protocol if reduced mobility.

**Return precautions communicated:** worsening dyspnea, chest pain at rest, confusion, persistent hypotension, inability to maintain SpO₂, or any allergic reaction signs.

*(ED Physician: Dr. Marko Jurić, 06.02.2026 21:02)*

- Additional safety concerns: ensure nitrate contraindications (PDE5 inhibitors) are checked; consider additional serial troponin/ECG given early presentation and borderline hs-troponin; caution with IV beta-blocker; anticoagulation should follow consideration of alternative lethal diagnoses (e.g., dissection) before heparin.

Not initiating a P2Y12 agent can increase risk of recurrent ischemia/infarction while awaiting inpatient management.

Contraindicated medication (potential): **IV metoprolol** in ACS can worsen outcomes if unrecognized LV dysfunction/incipient shock; ensure explicit screening for acute heart failure, bradycardia/AV block, RV infarct, or borderline perfusion and consider oral dosing if stable.  
Missed diagnosis (safety check before anticoagulation): Starting **heparin** without clearly addressing exclusion of **aortic dissection** (or other hemorrhage-prone causes of chest pain) can be catastrophic if present; ensure focused dissection screen (tearing pain/back radiation, neuro deficits, pulse/BP differential, mediastinal widening) and escalate imaging if any red flags.

error_explanation: The patient has multiple PE risk features (recent long-haul flight, estrogen-containing OCP), symptoms compatible with PE (sudden pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, tachycardia, initial borderline hypoxemia), and an elevated D-dimer. Declaring PE unlikely without applying a validated pretest probability tool (e.g., Wells/YEARS/PERC) and without appropriate imaging can miss a potentially life-threatening diagnosis.
correct_action: Formally assess PE pretest probability (Wells/YEARS). Given non-low probability features and elevated D-dimer, proceed to diagnostic imaging (CT pulmonary angiography; consider V/Q scan if CTPA contraindicated). Evaluate for DVT with compression ultrasound of the leg if indicated, and start anticoagulation if PE is strongly suspected and imaging is delayed/contraindicated, following local protocol.

gold_response:
<thinking>
Key safety issue: abrupt onset “spinning” vertigo + severe occipital headache + vomiting + inability to walk + focal cerebellar sign (left dysmetria) + transient slurred speech in a high vascular-risk 75F (HTN/DM/HLD, prior AF not anticoagulated). A normal non-contrast CT head does **not** rule out posterior circulation ischemic stroke/TIA. Stroke pathway/urgent vascular imaging and admission were indicated.
</thinking>

<primary_error>
FOUND: Yes  
TYPE: Missed diagnosis / Missing intervention  
QUOTE: "No indication for stroke pathway given normal CT head."  
PROBLEM: The presentation is high-risk for posterior circulation stroke/TIA (sudden vertigo, occipital headache, vomiting, gait inability, left dysmetria, reported transient dysarthria, multiple vascular risk factors). Non-contrast CT can be normal early in ischemic posterior fossa strokes, so using a negative CT to exclude stroke and discharge home creates a high risk of missed stroke with deterioration.  
ACTION: Activate stroke/TIA pathway: urgent neurology evaluation, NIHSS/targeted bedside oculomotor exam (HINTS by trained clinician), immediate vascular imaging (CTA head/neck ± CT perfusion) and/or MRI-DWI, plus hospital admission/observation for serial neuro exams and secondary prevention.
</primary_error>

<other_errors>
TYPE: Missing intervention: No CTA/MRA performed despite posterior circulation stroke red flags (focal cerebellar sign, gait not testable, transient dysarthria) — obtain CTA head/neck (evaluate basilar/vertebral occlusion/stenosis) and consider MRI-DWI if CTA negative but suspicion persists.  
TYPE: Potentially harmful management: BP lowered with IV labetalol in a patient who may have acute ischemic stroke; unless BP is extreme or thrombolysis planned, aggressive lowering can reduce cerebral perfusion — avoid acute BP reduction in suspected ischemic stroke unless >220/120 or other emergency indications; follow stroke BP protocols.  
TYPE: Unsafe disposition: Discharged while vertigo persisted and gait could not be safely assessed; plus spouse-reported neurologic symptom (slurred speech) — admit/observe for fall risk, evolving stroke, and repeat neuro exams/imaging.  
TYPE: Missed secondary prevention/risk assessment: History of paroxysmal AF “years ago” and not anticoagulated; if TIA/stroke suspected, this greatly increases recurrent stroke risk — perform telemetry/ECG review, consider prolonged rhythm monitoring, calculate CHA₂DS₂-VASc, and initiate appropriate antithrombotic/anticoagulation per neurology/cardiology after hemorrhage excluded and diagnosis clarified.
</other_errors>

<end_token>
error_location: “Neurological exam without focal deficit; symptoms most consistent with vestibular migraine. No indication for stroke pathway given normal CT head.”
error_explanation: This presentation (sudden onset vertigo + severe occipital headache + vomiting + gait inability + nystagmus + left dysmetria, with major vascular risk factors and possible transient slurred speech) is concerning for posterior circulation stroke (e.g., cerebellar infarct) or TIA. A normal non-contrast CT head does not exclude early ischemic stroke, especially in the posterior fossa. Documenting 'no indication for stroke pathway' based on CT alone overlooks an important diagnosis.
correct_action: Activate stroke evaluation: perform a focused bedside oculomotor exam (HINTS by trained clinician), obtain urgent vascular imaging (CTA head/neck) and/or MRI brain with DWI, consult neurology, and admit/observe for posterior circulation stroke. Assess atrial fibrillation history and consider anticoagulation/secondary prevention if stroke/TIA confirmed, and do not discharge until stroke is reasonably excluded.

report_content:
**Emergency Department Report (Synthetic, Realistic)**

**Patient:** M.K. (female)  
**DOB:** 1948-11-03 (77 years)  
**Arrival:** 2026-02-06 10:42 (walk-in, accompanied by daughter)

---

## Triage

**Triage Category:** 3 (urgent)  
**Chief Complaint:** Fever, right flank pain, dysuria

**Triage Data (10:46)**  
- BP: 104/62 mmHg  
- Pulse: 112 /min (regular)  
- SpO₂: 95% (room air)  
- RR: 20 /min  
- Temp: 38.6 °C (tympanic)  
- GCS: 15  
- Pain score: 7/10 (right flank)

---

## Reason for Admission
Suspected acute pyelonephritis with systemic inflammatory response.

---

## Anamnesis

**History of Present Illness:**  
2 days of burning urination and urinary frequency. Since last night, chills and fever, with increasing right-sided flank pain radiating to the groin. Nausea without vomiting. No chest pain or shortness of breath. No known kidney stones in the past. Took paracetamol at home with partial relief.

**Past Medical History:**  
- Mechanical mitral valve replacement (2016)  
- Chronic atrial fibrillation  
- Hypertension  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus  
- Stage 3 chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine ~120 µmol/L per patient card)  
- Hyperlipidemia

**Surgical History:** Mechanical valve (2016), cholecystectomy (2008)

**Epidemiological Anamnesis:** Sporadic, no travel, no known sick contacts.

**Habits:** Non-smoker, rare alcohol.

**Home Medications:**  
- **Warfarin** variable dosing (target INR 2.5–3.5 for mechanical valve per patient)  
- Bisoprolol 5 mg 1×1  
- Ramipril 5 mg 1×1  
- Metformin 500 mg 2×1  
- Atorvastatin 20 mg 1×1  
- Pantoprazole 20 mg 1×1

**Allergies:** Denies drug allergies.

---

## Physical Examination (11:05)

**General:** Ill-appearing, febrile, oriented, mildly dehydrated.  
**Skin/Mucosa:** Warm, dry mucosa.  
**Cardio:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic; mechanical click audible; no new murmurs. Peripheral pulses present.  
**Respiratory:** Vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles/wheezes.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness, no guarding.  
**Renal angle:** Marked right costovertebral angle tenderness.  
**Extremities:** No edema, no calf tenderness.  
**Neuro:** GCS 15, no focal deficit.

---

## Vital Signs (ED course)

| Date/Time | BP (mmHg) | Pulse (/min) | SpO₂ (%) | RR (/min) | Temp (°C) | GCS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 06.02.2026 10:46 | 104/62 | 112 | 95 | 20 | 38.6 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 12:10 | 110/66 | 104 | 96 | 18 | 38.0 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 14:30 | 116/70 | 92 | 97 | 16 | 37.6 | 15 |

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### Point-of-care / Urinalysis (11:20)
- Leukocyte esterase: **+++**
- Nitrite: **positive**
- Blood: **+**
- Protein: trace
- Ketones: negative
- Glucose: negative
- Specific gravity: 1.020

### Bloodwork (11:25)
**CBC**
- WBC **15.8 x10⁹/L** (NEU 88%)  
- HGB 122 g/L  
- Hct 0.368 L/L  
- Platelets 214 x10⁹/L

**Inflammation / Sepsis**
- CRP **164 mg/L**

**Renal / Electrolytes**
- Urea **12.8 mmol/L**

**Liver**
